canonicalFacts: SEP-0041 is titled `Soroban Token Interface` and defines a standard contract interface for tokens; current status is Draft (version 0.4.1 as of 2026-06-18).
SEP-41 is a standard/specification/interface, not a deployed token contract, not a SAC instance, and not the Stellar Asset Contract itself.
Per SEP-0041, the SEP-41 interface is a subset of the Stellar Asset Contract interface and is compatible with the interfaces defined in CAP-46-6.
Per Stellar docs, the SAC is an implementation of CAP-46-6 and SEP-41 for Stellar assets; an asset and its SAC represent the same asset (SAC is an API for the asset).
Contract tokens can implement SEP-41; contracts depending only on the SEP-41 portion of the SAC interface are compatible with any contract token that implements SEP-41.
Current Stellar docs prefer `contract token`; `custom token` is older/deprecated wording but still understandable.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Brief SAC context: SAC is built into/provided by Stellar and exposes Stellar assets to Soroban.
Brief note that SAC-backed assets preserve classic asset/trustline semantics (belongs mainly to Q10).
Brief OpenZeppelin/custom-token examples (belong mainly to Q11).
Rust trait/client names or method examples, if accurate and not treated as required.
ERC-20 analogy for interface intuition, if clearly only an analogy.

questions.question: Which Stellar standard(s) cover verifying that a deployed Soroban contract's WASM corresponds to its published source code, and what does that verification actually prove?

semantic.intent: Standards-dispatch test for Soroban contract WASM<->source verification. A good answer routes to the current Stellar standard(s) for establishing that deployed WASM corresponds to published source: SEP-58 (Contract Build Reproducibility for Verification) for independently rebuilding from source and comparing WASM bytes/hash, and/or SEP-55 (Contract Build Verification / listed as Contract Build Info) for GitHub-attestation-based build provenance. The single most important correctness point is the caveat that this proves build provenance / source reproducibility, not that the source is safe, audited, or non-malicious. Naming either standard correctly (with mechanism + caveat) is sufficient to pass; surfacing both and their distinction is full marks. Do not require a live/ecosystem tool: this is conceptual standards knowledge, not an index-freshness question.
mustInclude: Names at least one correct current standard for WASM<->source verification: SEP-58 (reproducible rebuild from source, compare WASM hash/bytes) and/or SEP-55 (GitHub-attestation-based contract build verification / build info / provenance).

Describes the mechanism for whichever it names: SEP-58 = rebuild the WASM in a pinned build environment from the source archive identified by `source_sha256` (and `source_uri` when present), then compare the rebuilt WASM SHA-256 to the deployed/original bytes; SEP-55 = a GitHub Actions attestation ties the deployed WASM hash to a source repo + commit, retrievable/inspectable downstream.

States the essential caveat that build/source verification proves how/where the WASM was built or that the source reproduces it, not that the source code is safe, audited, correct, or non-malicious; the source and workflow still need review.
mustAvoid: Do not claim SEP-55 or any build-verification standard proves the contract or source is safe, audited, trustworthy, or non-malicious.
Do not present SEP-46 Contract Meta as the verification standard; it is only the metadata carrier (the contractmetav0 custom section) the verification fields can live in.
Do not treat embedded contractmetav0 metadata as self-authenticating proof absent an attestation, a rebuild, or a hash comparison.
Do not misroute to unrelated standards as the answer: SEP-41 token interface, SAC, CAP-46, SEP-47/48 interface specs, or generic audits/formal verification.

Do not assert exact SEP status/version/title as fixed fact without qualifying that both are Draft and titles can drift (SEP-55 reads Contract Build Verification in its file but Contract Build Info in the ecosystem README).
niceToHave: Surfaces both SEP-58 and SEP-55 and distinguishes them: SEP-58 = independent rebuild/reproducibility (no trust in a CI, needs a deterministic build env); SEP-55 = attestation/provenance (trust the attesting CI, no rebuild); complementary, can coexist on one contract.
Mentions SEP-46 (Contract Meta) as the Active metadata mechanism (contractmetav0) the fields can be embedded in.

Mentions concrete fields/lookups: SEP-55 source_repo=github:<u>/<r> (+ home_domain) + the GitHub attestation lookup keyed by the WASM sha256; SEP-58 bldimg/bldopt + source_sha256 (required) / source_uri (optional).
Notes both are Draft as of 2026-06-19 (SEP-55 v0.4.1, SEP-58 v0.5.0) if making status/version claims.
Notes block explorers / Stellar Lab / verification registries may surface results, and that already-deployed contracts may need an on-chain registry or off-chain service if metadata was not embedded.
passIf: Answer names SEP-58 with the rebuild-and-compare mechanism and the safety caveat, even without mentioning SEP-55.
Answer names SEP-55 with the attestation/provenance mechanism and the safety caveat, even without mentioning SEP-58.
Answer names both SEPs and distinguishes rebuild-based from attestation-based verification (full marks).
A concise answer passes if it routes to the correct standard family, gives the verification mechanism, and includes the 'this is build/source provenance, not a safety proof' caveat.
failIf: Answer routes to SEP-41, SEP-46-as-the-verification-standard, SAC, CAP-46, or generic 'audit/formal verification' as the standard for WASM<->source verification.
Answer claims the standard proves the contract is safe/audited/non-malicious, or asserts it makes the contract trustworthy while omitting the limitation.
Answer treats contractmetav0 metadata as self-authenticating proof with no attestation, rebuild, or hash comparison.
Answer names no SEP and offers only generic 'source verification' with no mechanism.
inconclusiveIf: Answer names a correct SEP and its mechanism but omits the safety caveat (responsive but missing the key limitation the reworded question asks for).
Answer says 'contract build verification'/'source verification' and gestures at WASM-hash matching but names no SEP number and does not describe attestation vs rebuild.
Answer names SEP-46 plus source fields but never explains how verification (attestation, or rebuild + hash compare) is actually established.

preferredSources: stellar/stellar-protocol ecosystem/sep-0058.md
stellar/stellar-protocol ecosystem/sep-0055.md
stellar/stellar-protocol ecosystem/sep-0046.md
stellar/stellar-protocol ecosystem/README.md (status/title index)
Stellar Developers Contract Explorer / Lab docs for the Build Info caveat
evidenceRequirements.sourceUse: Citations optional for the stable dispatch + caveat. Exact status/version/title/current-name and field-vocabulary claims, such as SEP-58 source-identity fields, should cite or be checked against current stellar-protocol, because SEP-55/SEP-58 are Draft and naming/status/field sets can change. Do not penalize an answer for naming current SEP-58 fields that differ from an older snapshot. Official stellar-protocol files dominate local skill memory.

freshnessPolicy.classification: standards-dispatch question with draft-standard / status-sensitive edges
freshnessPolicy.rationale: The conceptual distinction (SEP-58 reproducible rebuild vs SEP-55 attestation provenance) and the safety caveat are stable enough to grade once encoded. But both standards are Draft and SEP-58 is recent (created 2026-05-01), so exact titles, versions, and status should be checked/qualified before strict grading.
freshnessPolicy.minimumFreshnessCheck: Before asserting exact current status/version/title, or claiming SEP-55 was replaced/renamed/deprecated, check current stellar-protocol SEP files + README. Do not require live tools for a conceptual answer that names SEP-58 and/or SEP-55 with their roles and the caveat.

canonicalFacts: SEP-0058 'Contract Build Reproducibility for Verification' (Leigh McCulloch) is Draft, v0.5.0, updated 2026-06-18. It defines a build-environment + source-identity vocabulary (`bldimg`, `bldopt`, required `source_sha256`, optional `source_uri`) so a third party can rebuild a contract's WASM from a source archive; the verification result is rebuilding in the recorded environment and comparing the rebuilt WASM SHA-256 to the original. It is storage-agnostic (contractmetav0, on-chain registry, or off-chain).

SEP-0055 is titled 'Contract Build Verification' in its file (Nando Vieira) but listed as 'Contract Build Info' in the ecosystem README; Draft, v0.4.1, updated 2025-03-12. It defines GitHub-Actions/GitHub-Attestations build provenance, stores source_repo=github:<u>/<r> (+ home_domain) in contractmetav0, and verifies via api.github.com/repos/<u>/<r>/attestations/sha256:<wasm_hash>.

SEP-55 and SEP-58 are complementary: SEP-55 answers 'did a trusted CI compile this WASM?' (trust the CI, no rebuild), while SEP-58 supports independently rebuilding from source and comparing bytes (no CI trust, needs a deterministic build env). SEP-58 references SEP-55; SEP-55 predates SEP-58 and may not reference it back. A contract can carry meta for both.
SEP-0046 'Contract Meta' (Leigh McCulloch) is Active; it defines the contractmetav0 WASM custom section that holds metadata entries. It is a carrier of claims, not proof by itself.

Both SEP-55 and SEP-58 state explicitly that build/reproducibility verification does not establish that the source is safe, audited, correct, or non-malicious; the source and workflow still need review.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Details of the SEP-55 GitHub attestation lookup endpoint or DSSE/in-toto payload fields.
Details of SEP-58 fields (`bldimg`, `bldopt`, `source_sha256`, `source_uri`) and the docker-run reproducible-build flow.
Mention of Stellar Lab / Contract Explorer displaying Build Info, with the caveat that users still review source/workflow.
Mention that already-deployed contracts may need an external registry / off-chain service if metadata was not embedded.
Brief note that a contract can carry both SEP-55 and SEP-58-compatible metadata.

questions.question: I want users to sign Stellar dApp actions with passkeys instead of seed phrases. What pieces do I need and what should I be careful about?

answerRegex: smart account|smart wallet|contract account|wallet contract|custom account|__check_auth

semantic.intent: Test whether the answer can explain how to build Stellar dApp actions authorized by passkeys instead of user-held seed phrases. A good answer should identify the contract-account/smart-wallet architecture, WebAuthn/passkey credential flow, CAP-0051/secp256r1 verification in __check_auth, Soroban auth-entry signing/submission mechanics, and the security/UX caveats around fee sponsorship, policies, replay resistance, and recovery.
mustInclude: States that users need a Soroban contract account / smart account / smart wallet that holds assets and implements authorization logic in __check_auth rather than relying on a classic seed phrase account for user signing.

Mentions WebAuthn/passkeys as the browser/platform credential flow: register a credential/device keypair, store the public key and usually credential ID or signer metadata, then request an assertion/signature when the user approves a dApp action.
Mentions secp256r1 / P-256 / ES256 / CAP-0051 / Protocol 21 native verification as the Stellar/Soroban primitive that makes common WebAuthn passkey signatures verifiable on-chain.

Explains that dApp contract calls requiring authorization use Soroban authorization/auth entries; C-account smart wallets sign/approve auth entries according to their contract logic and cannot sign classic transaction envelopes as the transaction source.

Mentions transaction submission and fees: a separate G-account/source/fee payer, relayer, OpenZeppelin Relayer/Channels, fee bump, or fee sponsorship is commonly needed because C-accounts cannot directly be the transaction source/pay fees and users may not hold XLM.

Includes at least one concrete caveat from either family: a policy/recovery caveat such as spend limits, allow lists, timelocks, session keys, policy/multisig signers, recovery/admin keys, signer rotation, or testing allow and deny paths; or a WebAuthn/auth caveat such as origin/RP ID binding, WebAuthn challenge/payload binding to the Soroban authorization signature payload/preimage derived from the auth entry and invocation tree, replay/nonce/signature-expiration-ledger, credential-clone/counter, or secure credential storage.
mustAvoid: Do not present passkeys as ordinary Stellar ed25519 account seed phrases or claim passkeys sign classic Stellar transaction envelopes directly for C-accounts.
Do not omit the contract-account / __check_auth layer and describe only frontend login/authentication.
Do not imply a relayer/fee sponsor can authorize user funds; the user/passkey/account contract authorizes the action, while the relayer/fee payer submits/pays fees.

Do not recommend Launchtube as the current primary production path without qualifying it as legacy/archived/superseded; it may be mentioned if status is qualified and the current OpenZeppelin Relayer + Stellar Channels Service path is named.
Do not claim passkeys automatically make the wallet safe, recoverable, phishing-proof, or custody-free without contract policies and recovery design.
Do not ignore network/protocol/tooling support for secp256r1/CAP-0051 when making production-readiness claims.
niceToHave: Mentions Smart Account Kit as a current TypeScript SDK for OpenZeppelin smart accounts on Stellar/Soroban, while not making it mandatory if a custom or OpenZeppelin-contracts-based implementation is described.
Mentions OpenZeppelin Stellar Contracts smart accounts, signers, policies, context rules, or policy signers.
Mentions Soroban RPC simulation/assembly, recording vs enforcing auth, signAuthEntry, and short signatureExpirationLedger values.
Mentions fee-bump transactions or sponsored reserves separately from relayer transaction submission where relevant.
Mentions account discovery/indexing and storage of contract IDs, credential IDs, public keys, and session state.
Mentions fallback/recovery options such as backup passkeys, Ed25519 admin/recovery signer, multisig, guardian, or policy-controlled signer changes.
Mentions detailed WebAuthn security caveats such as challenge/origin/RP ID binding, replay resistance, counters/clone detection, payload binding, and tests for malicious/nested subinvocations.
passIf: Answer gives the architecture: passkey/WebAuthn frontend + Soroban smart/contract account implementing __check_auth + secp256r1/CAP-0051 verification + WebAuthn challenge bound to the Soroban authorization signature payload/preimage + auth-entry signing/submission + fee-payer/relayer considerations + concrete security/recovery caveats.
A concise answer passes if it clearly distinguishes user authorization from transaction fee submission and names the contract account/auth-entry model for C-accounts.
An answer can pass without naming Smart Account Kit if it accurately describes the underlying pieces and caveats.
failIf: Answer says users can simply use passkeys as seed phrases or replace the Stellar secret key with a passkey without smart/contract account authorization.
Answer is only generic WebAuthn login advice and does not mention Stellar/Soroban contract accounts, __check_auth, or auth entries.
Answer omits secp256r1/P-256/ES256/CAP-0051/Protocol 21 or any equivalent explanation of how WebAuthn signatures are verified on Stellar.
Answer omits the C-account transaction-source limitation or fee-payer/relayer/fee-sponsorship need while presenting the flow as complete for dApp actions.
Answer recommends Launchtube as the production/current relayer path without any current-status qualification.
Answer claims passkeys alone solve recovery/phishing/policy/security risks or says no recovery/policy design is needed.
inconclusiveIf: Answer mentions passkeys and smart wallets but does not clearly explain __check_auth or auth-entry signing.
Answer accurately describes Smart Account Kit/OpenZeppelin tooling but omits underlying CAP-0051/secp256r1 or C-account fee-payer mechanics.
Answer explains contract-account/passkey mechanics but omits practical caveats around WebAuthn challenge/auth-payload binding, replay/expiration, origin, recovery, or policies.
Answer is correct for a specific SDK tutorial but too SDK-specific to show the general pieces a builder needs.

preferredSources: Stellar Developers Contract Accounts and Smart wallets docs
Stellar Developers Signing Soroban contract invocations guide
stellar/stellar-protocol CAP-0051 and core README
Stellar Developers OpenZeppelin Relayer docs
OpenZeppelin Relayer / Stellar Channels Service guide
stellar/launchtube archive banner for Launchtube legacy status
OpenZeppelin Stellar Contracts / Smart Account Kit docs or repository when naming current tooling
evidenceRequirements.sourceUse: Use official Stellar docs and stellar-protocol as source truth for contract-account semantics, CAP-0051 status, auth-entry signing, and C-account limitations. Use current tooling docs/repos for SDK/relayer recommendations, and do not assume all published Stellar docs are equally fresh on Launchtube; the launchtube archive banner and OpenZeppelin Relayer/Channels docs are the current-status sources of truth for that tooling edge.

freshnessPolicy.classification: implementation-sensitive dApp architecture question with stable protocol core and volatile tooling edges
freshnessPolicy.rationale: The core model is stable, but current docs/tooling around passkey kits, OpenZeppelin smart accounts, relayers, and Launchtube replacement are changing. The Stellar signing guide was updated 2026-06-17, so stale answers are plausible and should be gated by live source use.
freshnessPolicy.minimumFreshnessCheck: Before recommending a named SDK, relayer, hosted service, or claiming production readiness/deprecation status, check current official Stellar docs and relevant project docs. CAP-0051 status should be checked against stellar-protocol for exact status/protocol-version claims.

canonicalFacts: Contract accounts are smart contracts that act like accounts and use __check_auth to decide who can act and under what conditions.
Smart wallets are contract accounts used as user wallets; passkeys/WebAuthn are common signers, but Ed25519 keys, policy signers, session keys, and custom verifier logic are also possible.

CAP-0051 is Final and Protocol 21; it adds native secp256r1/P-256 verification to Soroban, enabling efficient on-chain verification of common WebAuthn/passkey signatures. The WebAuthn challenge should bind to the SHA-256 of the ENVELOPE_TYPE_SOROBAN_AUTHORIZATION preimage — the Soroban authorization signature payload over network id, nonce, signatureExpirationLedger, and the invocation tree of the auth entry — not to an unrelated login challenge or classic transaction envelope.

C-account smart wallets cannot sign transaction envelopes or be transaction source accounts; they authorize Soroban invocations by signing/authenticating auth entries, and a separate G-account source/fee payer submits/pays fees.
Relayers/fee sponsorship/fee-bump/channel services improve UX and may be required for practical C-account flows, but they do not replace user authorization by the passkey/account contract.

The stellar/launchtube repo was archived on 2026-03-09 and superseded by OpenZeppelin Relayer with the Channels Plugin / Stellar Channels Service. Some developers.stellar.org smart-wallet/passkey pages still present Launchtube as current, so answers must qualify the status rather than blindly trust those pages.

Security depends on correct WebAuthn challenge/origin/payload binding, replay protection, signature expiration, signer/policy checks, recovery paths, and tests for denied as well as allowed authorization.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Detailed Smart Account Kit setup code or package names.
Detailed OpenZeppelin Stellar Contracts account/signer/policy module names.
Explanation of WebAuthn authenticator data, clientDataJSON, RP ID/origin, credential ID, signature counters, or DER-to-compact signature conversion.
Discussion of sponsored reserves for account/trustline minimum balances in addition to transaction fee sponsorship.
Mention of wallet adapters such as Freighter/LOBSTR/Wallets Kit as complementary options, as long as they do not replace the passkey smart-account architecture.
Mention of Passkey Kit as legacy, demo/unaudited, or simpler tooling, if current status is qualified.

questions.question: How do I charge AI agents per API call on Stellar?

semantic.intent: Test whether the answer can explain how to charge autonomous agents per API request on Stellar using the current agentic-payments stack. A good answer should route between x402 for HTTP 402 paid-resource flows and MPP for Stellar-native machine payments, identify the Stellar-specific payment/authentication mechanics, explain verification/settlement before access, and avoid generic off-chain billing or non-Stellar x402 guidance.
mustInclude: Identifies x402 and/or MPP as the relevant Stellar agentic-payments pattern for per-request API charging rather than ordinary API keys, subscriptions, Stripe-only billing, or manual invoices.

For x402, explains the HTTP 402 flow: protected API returns a payment-required challenge/requirements, the agent/client submits a payment payload/signature, the server or facilitator verifies and settles it, then access is granted.

Mentions at least one Stellar-specific payment primitive: Soroban authorization/auth entries for x402, SEP-41 tokens, Stellar Asset Contract/SAC transfers, USDC on Stellar, or Stellar network identifiers such as stellar:testnet or stellar:pubnet.

Explains the role of a Stellar facilitator/relayer or equivalent server-side settlement path for x402, including verifying the payment payload and submitting/settling the transfer before serving the paid response.

Distinguishes x402 from MPP when relevant: x402 is the HTTP 402/facilitator-friendly API-payment protocol, while MPP can charge directly with Soroban SAC transfers and can use charge mode or session/channel mode for repeated or high-frequency interactions.
mustAvoid: Do not answer only with EVM/Base/Solana x402 details or Coinbase facilitator assumptions without adapting the flow to Stellar/Soroban.
Do not present the solution as generic API-key metering, Stripe/credit-card billing, off-chain credits, or database counters without an on-chain Stellar payment/settlement step.
Do not say Stellar x402 uses pre-signed transaction envelopes instead of Soroban authorization entries when discussing the x402 protocol specifically.
Do not imply API access should be granted before the payment payload has been verified and, where required, settled/submitted.
Do not claim XLM is the only usable asset; Stellar x402/MPP flows commonly use SEP-41/SAC tokens and USDC, though exact current asset support should be live-checked.

Do not provide exact npm package names, import paths, SDK helper names, facilitator URLs, API-key generation URLs, or hard-coded asset issuer/contract IDs as copy-paste facts unless the trace contains current official docs or package-source evidence for those exact details.
Do not call x402 the only supported pattern or promise zero-XLM clients as a blanket fact; qualify fee sponsorship and support by current facilitator/relayer docs.
niceToHave: Mentions current Stellar x402 facilitator options and qualifies network support, such as Coinbase being testnet-only if that remains current and Built-on-Stellar/OpenZeppelin relayer paths covering testnet/mainnet if current docs confirm it.
Mentions x402 scheme/network metadata such as exact-v2 and stellar:testnet or stellar:pubnet when current docs require it.

Mentions wallet/client support for Soroban auth-entry signing, such as Freighter browser extension, Albedo, Hana, HOT, Klever, or OneKey, and qualifies mobile or wallet limitations only after live checking.
Mentions sponsored fees, relayers, or OpenZeppelin Channels/Relayer as UX infrastructure that can keep agents from managing native fee balances.
Mentions trustlines, SAC wrapping, contract IDs, or asset support caveats for issued assets such as USDC.

Includes a concrete implementation sketch such as middleware guarding an endpoint, a /verify and /settle call sequence, or an MPP charge/session flow. Pseudocode is acceptable and often preferable unless exact package/API details are officially verified in the trace.
passIf: Answer explains a current Stellar x402 paid-API flow with HTTP 402 challenge, Soroban auth-entry based payment, facilitator/relayer verification and settlement, and token/payment asset handling before access.

Answer can pass by recommending MPP instead of x402 if it clearly describes per-request Stellar SAC transfers or MPP charge/session mechanics and the question is answered as a Stellar-native machine-payment API design.

A concise answer passes if it names x402 or MPP, includes the 402/per-request/API charging idea, includes a Stellar-specific payment primitive, and states that payment must be verified/settled before serving the API result.
failIf: Answer is an EVM/Base/Solana-only x402 answer that does not explain the Stellar/Soroban adaptation.
Answer omits Stellar-specific payment mechanics entirely and gives only generic SaaS billing, API-key, webhook, or database-metering advice.
Answer discusses Stellar payments but does not connect them to per-request/API access control, HTTP 402, x402, MPP, or an equivalent machine-payment gate.
Answer says x402 on Stellar is just signing and submitting normal Stellar transaction envelopes and does not mention Soroban auth entries or an equivalent x402 auth-entry payment payload.
Answer recommends granting access optimistically before verification/settlement as the main design without caveats.
Answer gives stale or unqualified facilitator, wallet, endpoint, or asset-support claims that contradict current official docs in a way that would break implementation.
Answer gives concrete copy-paste package/import/facilitator/asset details without official-doc or package-source evidence, and presents them as current rather than illustrative placeholders.
inconclusiveIf: Answer names x402 and Stellar but is too high-level to tell whether payment verification and settlement happen before API access.

Answer accurately describes MPP Charge and says the client signs Soroban auth entries OR signs/sends a transaction envelope; current Stellar MPP docs describe both for default Pull, so either is correct and must not be marked wrong merely for failing to mention x402 auth entries or for choosing the other Pull characterization.
Answer gives a plausible facilitator-based flow but omits whether the payment is USDC, SEP-41, SAC, or another Stellar token.
Answer is mostly correct but names current facilitator, wallet, or endpoint support without enough live-source evidence to verify freshness.
Answer compares Stellar x402 to Base/EVM/Solana x402 but does not clearly state which parts are Stellar-specific.
Answer includes illustrative package/API pseudocode with clear live-check caveats; this should not fail if the protocol flow is correct, but should not be treated as fully copy-paste ready.

preferredSources: Stellar Developers x402 docs
Stellar Developers x402 Built on Stellar / facilitator flow docs
Stellar Developers MPP docs and charge/session guides
x402 protocol documentation for HTTP 402 challenge, verify, settle, and supported-resource semantics
OpenZeppelin Relayer / Built on Stellar x402 facilitator docs when recommending that path
Stellar Developers SAC, token interface, or SEP-0041 docs when discussing token asset mechanics
Current package repositories or docs for named SDKs, middleware, or facilitator endpoints
evidenceRequirements.sourceUse: Use current official Stellar docs as source truth for Stellar-specific x402, MPP, auth-entry, facilitator, network, asset, and wallet support claims. Use protocol docs for generic x402 HTTP semantics. Treat exact endpoints, supported wallets, facilitator availability, asset contract IDs, package names, and network support as live facts rather than memory.

freshnessPolicy.classification: implementation-sensitive agentic-payments question with stable protocol intent and volatile tooling/facilitator edges
freshnessPolicy.rationale: The high-level idea of HTTP 402 and Stellar machine payments is stable, but the practical answer depends on current facilitator availability, x402 scheme versions, Stellar network identifiers, USDC/SEP-41/SAC support, wallet auth-entry support, package names, and relayer endpoints.
freshnessPolicy.minimumFreshnessCheck: Before recommending a named facilitator, endpoint, SDK, wallet, asset contract ID, or mainnet/testnet capability, check current Stellar and provider docs. Stale claims about Coinbase vs Built-on-Stellar/OpenZeppelin facilitator support, Freighter Mobile support, or network availability should be treated as misses or inconclusive depending on severity.

canonicalFacts: x402 is an HTTP-native payment protocol for paid resources using 402 Payment Required: the server returns payment requirements, the client/agent submits a payment payload, the server or facilitator verifies and settles, then the paid response is returned.

On Stellar, x402 uses Soroban authorization / signed auth entries rather than pre-signed normal transaction envelopes, which avoids sequence-number conflicts and supports fee sponsorship and replay protection by expiration.
Stellar x402 payments can use SEP-41/SAC tokens, with USDC commonly used in current docs; exact asset and network support should be live-checked.

A Stellar x402 facilitator verifies the network/scheme/payment payload, decodes the Soroban XDR/payment details, checks token transfer details and payer authorization, simulates or validates the operation, and settles/submits the payment before the API grants access.

MPP is a related Stellar-native machine-payment protocol: Charge mode can do one on-chain SAC transfer per request, while Session/Channel mode can use off-chain cumulative commitments and settle later for high-frequency sessions.

Signing differs by protocol: x402 on Stellar signs Soroban authorization entries and should not be described as pre-signed transaction envelopes. For MPP Charge, current official Stellar docs are internally inconsistent about default Pull mode: the MPP overview page describes the client signing Soroban authorization entries while the MPP Charge guide describes the client signing the full transaction envelope, so accept either. The sponsored-fee Pull path signs only auth entries and lets the server rebuild with its own source account; Push mode has the client broadcast the transaction itself and send a signedHash credential rather than auth entries.
Current facilitator, wallet, package, endpoint, and supported-network details are volatile and must be checked live before making implementation recommendations.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Code snippets for Express, Hono, Workers, or another middleware that enforces an x402 payment gate.
Detailed /verify, /settle, or /supported endpoint request-response flow.
Specific current facilitator names, package names, API key setup, or environment variables, if live-sourced.
Detailed discussion of MPP Charge Pull, Push, sponsored flows, Session/Channel mode, or mppx.
Discussion of trustlines, reserve requirements, fee sponsorship, or account funding for agents and recipients.
Comparison to EVM/Base/Solana x402 as long as the Stellar/Soroban flow remains correct and primary.

questions.question: Before giving me ecosystem recommendations, check whether the Stellar ecosystem index is fresh enough to rely on. What does the index say about its project count and last-updated status?

semantic.intent: Check the live Stellar ecosystem index's own freshness/status before relying on it for ecosystem recommendations, and report what the status output says about the project count and project-data freshness.
mustInclude: Uses the live ecosystem index status/freshness tool output as the source of truth, not memory, static docs, or general web research.
Reports the current project count from the projects source in the status output, without hard-coding or inventing a fixed expected value.

Reports a concrete freshness timestamp or status for the projects data, such as the projects source last-updated time, read from the live status output, or clearly states if that value is missing/null.
Gives a concise reliance judgment for recommendations, such as fresh enough to use, stale, or usable with caveats, based on the returned count and freshness evidence.
mustAvoid: Do not answer from recall, stale saved counts, public web docs, or general web search instead of the live status output.
Do not hard-code a project count or last-updated date as a permanent canonical fact; these values are dynamic.
Do not give only a vague freshness claim such as 'it looks fresh' without the project count and timestamp/status evidence.
Do not confuse the overall status generated/as-of timestamp with the projects source last-updated timestamp; both can be mentioned, but they are different fields with different meanings.

Do not present null timestamps for other sources, such as hackathons or sdfSkills, as if the projects source itself is missing freshness data unless the projects source actually has null/missing freshness data.

Do not expose raw internal tool names, raw API endpoint paths, private provider names, raw status/source keys such as ecosystemStats or sdfSkills, or raw implementation mechanics in the final user-visible answer. Use neutral wording such as 'the live Stellar ecosystem index' or a public page if citing.
niceToHave: Also reports the overall status snapshot generated/as-of time and distinguishes it from the projects last-updated time.

Mentions other returned source statuses only as caveats in neutral prose, for example the builder directory's count/last-updated status, the third-party developer-activity snapshot's as-of date, or sources whose last-updated value is null, without printing raw field labels such as ecosystemStats, sdfSkills, or builders.

passIf: A concise answer says the live ecosystem index reports a project count, gives the projects freshness timestamp/status from the status output, and states whether that is fresh enough to rely on for recommendations.

failIf: The trace does not include the ecosystem index status/freshness tool call.

The answer relies on general web research, docs, or memory while ignoring the status output.

The answer gives the generated/as-of time but is ambiguous about whether the projects source last-updated timestamp/status was checked.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: Project count and last-updated/generated timestamps can change daily or intra-day. The golden question should evaluate whether the agent checks and reports the live status output, not whether it matches a fixed count/date.

The exact projects count and freshness timestamp are dynamic and should be read from the tool output for the current run.

questions.question: Who is building wallet infrastructure on Stellar right now - wallets, wallet integration tooling, or smart-account/passkey layers? Name a few current projects and say what each builds.

answerRegex: freighter|lobstr|xbull|hana|albedo|rabet|beans|vesseo|solar wallet|stellar passport|unstoppable wallet|lumexo|stellar wallets? kit|passkey kit|smart wallets?|smart account kit|latch|creit|walletconnect|hot wallet

semantic.intent: Answer the current Stellar wallet-infrastructure landscape question using the live ecosystem project index as primary evidence. Name several concrete projects or teams building wallet products, wallet integration tooling, or smart-account/passkey infrastructure, and briefly state what each is building. Prioritize Stellar-native or Stellar-first wallet infrastructure; multi-chain custody or embedded-wallet platforms that merely list Stellar as a supported chain are acceptable only when named as such, not as the core of the landscape.
mustInclude: Uses a current local ecosystem project/directory source in the trace, preferably a query such as wallet, wallet infrastructure, wallet kit, passkey, smart account, custody, embedded wallet, or a specific project follow-up.
Names at least three concrete Stellar-relevant projects/teams and gives a short role/category for each rather than just listing names.

Includes at least one Stellar-native wallet or Stellar wallet tooling example, such as Freighter, LOBSTR, xBull, Hana, Albedo, Rabet, Beans, Vesseo, Solar Wallet, Stellar Passport, Stellar Wallets Kit/Creit, Passkey Kit/smart wallets, or Latch, when the answer also lists multi-chain custody or embedded-wallet providers.

Includes at least one infrastructure, tooling, smart-account, passkey, embedded, or custody example when the answer also lists end-user wallets, or explicitly explains that it is using a broader wallet-infrastructure definition.
Qualifies the list as examples from the currently checked ecosystem index rather than an exhaustive canonical ranking.
mustAvoid: Do not present a stale fixed wallet list as exhaustive or authoritative for 'right now'.
Do not treat consumer wallets alone as the whole wallet-infrastructure landscape unless the answer clearly scopes itself to user-facing wallets and still names multiple current examples.

Do not present multi-chain custody/MPC/embedded-wallet platforms, such as BitGo, DFNS, Fordefi, MPCVault, Privy, or Arculus, that merely support Stellar as the primary Stellar wallet-infrastructure builders without flagging them as multi-chain integrations.
Do not conflate 'supports Stellar' with 'building wallet infrastructure on Stellar'.
Do not name projects as Stellar wallet infrastructure without either trace evidence or a clear public source showing Stellar relevance.
Do not expose raw internal tool names, endpoints, provider mechanics, or private implementation details in user-visible text.
passIf: A concise answer uses the live project index/directory, names a few current examples such as Freighter, LOBSTR, xBull, Hana, Albedo, Rabet, Beans, Vesseo, Solar Wallet, Stellar Passport, Stellar Wallets Kit/Creit, Passkey Kit, Latch, Lumexo, Unstoppable Wallet, WalletConnect, or Hot Wallet when supported by the trace, and says what category each belongs to.
A stronger answer separates categories, for example: end-user wallets, multi-wallet adapter kits, embedded/custody/key-management platforms, and smart-account/passkey infrastructure.
The answer may include public project/docs links as corroboration, but the core project recommendations are grounded in the local live ecosystem index.
failIf: The trace does not include search_projects or search_directory for this current ecosystem-landscape question.
The answer gives generic categories with no concrete project/team names.
The answer lists only one project or gives only a memorized legacy list with no current/source-grounded evidence.
The answer claims there are no current wallet-infrastructure builders without a dedicated and broad live search that could reasonably support that absence claim.
The answer's landscape is composed mainly of multi-chain custody/embedded providers presented as Stellar-native wallet infrastructure, with no Stellar-native wallet or Stellar wallet tooling named.
The answer materially misclassifies unrelated projects as Stellar wallet infrastructure or invents project capabilities not supported by trace/public evidence.
The answer leaks internal tool names, raw API paths, or provider implementation details.
inconclusiveIf: The answer names plausible current projects but is too terse to verify what each builds.
The trace uses only get_builders or only web research and the answer is plausible, but project-level grounding in the local ecosystem index is missing.
The local index returns sparse or conflicting results and the answer honestly scopes the uncertainty rather than fabricating a definitive landscape.
The answer includes only end-user wallets but otherwise appears current and sourced; judge should prefer inconclusive over fail unless it explicitly claims to cover infrastructure broadly.
The answer names multi-chain custody or embedded-wallet providers with Stellar support but does not clearly distinguish them from Stellar-native wallet infrastructure.

preferredSources: The live Stellar ecosystem project index/directory results for wallet, wallet infrastructure, wallet kit, passkey, smart account, embedded wallet, custody, or specific project queries
Public project pages or official Stellar/OpenZeppelin/Privy docs only as corroboration for project category and current Stellar relevance
evidenceRequirements.sourceUse: The local live ecosystem index/directory is primary for current project recommendations. Public docs/pages may corroborate category details, but should not replace local index evidence for this golden. Judge project claims against trace evidence available for that run; do not require a fixed canonical list.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The set of wallet, embedded-wallet, custody, adapter, and smart-account projects changes as integrations launch or go stale. The eval should reward live project discovery and honest scoping rather than memorized wallet lists.
freshnessPolicy.minimumFreshnessCheck: Call a local ecosystem project/directory search during the turn and ground named current projects in those returned results, or clearly say when the index is insufficient.

canonicalFacts: Stellar wallet infrastructure is broader than consumer wallets; it can include wallet products, multi-wallet adapter kits, embedded/custody/key-management providers, and smart-account/passkey tooling when Stellar relevance is sourced.

Live index behavior observed on 2026-06-18: a wallet query returned Stellar-native consumer wallets such as LOBSTR, xBull, Freighter, Hana, Beans, Albedo, Rabet, Vesseo, Solar Wallet, and Stellar Passport with descriptions; a wallet infrastructure query was dominated by multi-chain custody/security providers such as MPCVault, DFNS, BitGo, Fordefi, Arculus, Cactus Link, human.tech, and WalletConnect that support Stellar but should not be treated as the core Stellar-native wallet-infra landscape.

Current public Stellar developer docs list Stellar Wallets Kit as a multi-wallet integration layer supporting wallets such as Albedo, Freighter, Hana, LOBSTR, Rabet, WalletConnect, xBull, HOT Wallet, and hardware wallets.

OpenZeppelin Stellar smart accounts are contract-based wallets with programmable authorization via signers, context rules, policies, and verifiers, so smart-account tooling can be relevant wallet infrastructure when the answer explains that scope.
The exact best examples are dynamic; names in this rubric are allowed examples, not a mandatory or exhaustive list.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Links to project pages or public docs for named examples.
A note that some examples are consumer wallets while others are infrastructure/tooling providers or multi-chain providers with Stellar support.
A next step such as checking individual project repos/docs for production readiness before integrating.
Mention of SCF funding or status if present in the trace, provided it is not invented or over-weighted.
Mention of custody, MPC, embedded wallet, or institutional providers if they are clearly labeled as multi-chain or Stellar-supporting rather than the core Stellar-native wallet set.

questions.question: Are there any open SCF RFPs right now, and are any Stellar hackathons active or upcoming as of today?

semantic.intent: Answer a current, two-part opportunity-status question: whether any SCF RFPs are open right now, and whether any Stellar hackathons are active or upcoming as of the question date. Use the live local RFP and hackathon feeds as primary evidence, summarize the current result honestly, and avoid exposing internal tool names or raw API paths. Q19 separately handles detailed active ZK hackathon metadata, so Q18 should remain a broader RFP plus hackathon snapshot.
mustInclude: Uses live RFP evidence from get_rfps and live hackathon evidence from get_hackathons in the trace.
Explicitly answers the SCF RFP side: whether open RFPs exist right now, with either a count and representative names if present or a scoped no-open-RFP statement if none are returned.
Explicitly answers the hackathon side: whether any Stellar hackathons are active or upcoming today, distinguishing active from upcoming when the evidence supports it.
Dates or qualifies the status snapshot, for example 'as of today' or 'as of the live check', because open/active/upcoming statuses are volatile.
mustAvoid: Do not answer from memory for this current-status question.
Do not hard-code old event or RFP names as if they are current unless they appear in the live tool/source evidence for that run.
Do not claim no open RFPs or no active/upcoming hackathons unless the corresponding live feed was checked and the absence is scoped to that check.
Do not treat completed hackathons as active/upcoming unless the live status says active or upcoming.
Do not expose raw internal tool names, raw API endpoints, provider mechanics, or internal field labels in user-visible text.
Prefer external/public upstreams, such as DoraHacks or communityfund.stellar.org/SCF, or a public page URL over writing internal provider brand wording in prose.
niceToHave: Where useful, attributes the snapshot to a user-facing source, such as a public page URL, an external DoraHacks listing, an SCF/community-fund page, or a generic source name; a terse answer correctly grounded in the live trace should not fail solely for omitting a clickable link.
Prize pool, organizer, deadline, participant/hacker count, funding-eligibility, or category details for returned hackathons/RFPs when present in the evidence.
A next action such as opening the RFP idea page or DoraHacks page to apply/submit.
A brief note that completed hackathons are excluded from active/upcoming status.
Mention of fallback announcement channels only when no active/upcoming hackathons are returned or when the tool provides them.
passIf: The answer calls both required local tools, reports the current RFP and hackathon statuses from those outputs, and gives concise source-backed details for any open/active/upcoming items found.
If open RFPs exist, the answer names a few or gives the count rather than vaguely saying opportunities exist.
If no RFPs or no active/upcoming hackathons are returned, the answer says that clearly and scopes the absence to the live check instead of claiming universal nonexistence.
The answer may mention the active ZK hackathon if it is in the live results, but does not make the whole Q18 evaluation depend on that exact event name when future tool output differs.
failIf: The trace is missing either get_rfps or get_hackathons.
The answer addresses only RFPs or only hackathons.
The answer states stale or unsupported current statuses, such as claiming a completed hackathon is active or claiming no open RFPs when live RFP evidence shows open items.
The answer gives a flat absence claim without the corresponding live check.
The answer leaks internal tool names, raw API paths, or private provider/source mechanics.
inconclusiveIf: One or both live tools return an error or sparse/conflicting data and the answer honestly reports the limitation while still answering as far as the evidence allows.
The answer is plausible and uses both tools, but is too terse to verify whether the returned statuses were interpreted correctly.
Public sources conflict with the local feed and the answer surfaces the conflict instead of fabricating a definitive status.

preferredSources: The live local RFP feed, especially open/current-quarter RFP results and public RFP/ideas page URLs
The live local hackathon feed, especially active/upcoming status, start/end dates, organizer, and public DoraHacks or Stellar hackathon URLs
Public SCF handbook or communityfund.stellar.org pages only as corroboration for RFP-track rules, not as a replacement for the live current-status feed when stale or inconsistent
evidenceRequirements.sourceUse: Local live feeds are primary for volatile status. Public pages are useful corroboration and user-facing citations. Judge against the tool output available for that run; do not require the 2026-06-19 event/RFP names if the live feed changes later.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: RFP open status and hackathon active/upcoming status can change with round deadlines and event dates. Correctness depends on the date of the check, not memory.
freshnessPolicy.minimumFreshnessCheck: Call get_rfps and get_hackathons during the turn and answer both sides from those results; date or qualify the snapshot.

canonicalFacts: As of a live check on 2026-06-19, get_rfps(status=open) returned five open Q2 2026 RFPs: Trustline Onboarder, Passkey UI Kit, Account Demolisher, Contract Source Verification Service, and OZ Accounts Policy Builder, with public source https://stellarlight.xyz/ideas.

As of a live check on 2026-06-19, get_hackathons(status=active) returned one active hackathon, Stellar Hacks: Real-World ZK, running 2026-06-15 through 2026-06-29 with DoraHacks source https://dorahacks.io/hackathon/stellar-hacks-zk/detail; get_hackathons(status=upcoming) returned zero upcoming events.
Those 2026-06-19 names and counts are observed facts for this audit, not permanent expected answers. Future runs should be judged against contemporaneous tool output.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Clickable public source links for returned RFPs or hackathons.
Prize pool, organizer, deadline, participant/hacker count, funding-eligibility, or category details for returned hackathons/RFPs when present in the evidence.
A next action such as opening the RFP idea page or DoraHacks page to apply/submit.
A brief note that completed hackathons are excluded from active/upcoming status.
Mention of fallback announcement channels only when no active/upcoming hackathons are returned or when the tool provides them.

questions.question: Is there an open Stellar RFP related to Soroban contract source verification? Summarize what it asks builders to create.

The answer uses get_rfps and is directionally plausible but too terse to verify whether it matched the contract-source-verification RFP versus another developer-tooling/infrastructure RFP.

preferredSources: The live local RFP feed filtered or interpreted for open/current RFP status, especially title, id/slug, status, quarter, description, technical requirements, and public URL fields

Do not expose raw internal tool names, raw API endpoints, provider mechanics, or internal field labels in user-visible text. Public DoraHacks event URLs are acceptable user-facing sources.
niceToHave: Gives the date/status context for each event: Real-World ZK active June 15-29, 2026 and Agents completed March 30-April 13, 2026, when present in evidence.

Mentions x402, Stripe, and MPP as the theme/context for the Agents event.
passIf: The trace includes compare_hackathons and the answer correctly compares the two target events by registered hacker count and prize pool.

A complete answer based on get_hackathons only can be partner-ready if the trace contains both target events with their count and prize fields, but it should still miss the deterministic compare_hackathons routing gate.
For the 2026-06-19 observed case, the answer says Stellar Hacks: Agents had more registered hackers, 591 vs 295 for Stellar Hacks: Real-World ZK, and that both prize pools were $10,000.

While Real-World ZK is still active it can overtake the Agents event; if the contemporaneous live comparison shows Real-World ZK now has more registered hackers, an answer reporting ZK as the current leader is correct and passes, provided it gives both counts and both $10,000 prize pools.
A concise answer passes if it clearly names both events, gives both counts and both prize pools, and states the correct winner by registered hackers.
If future live evidence changes the Real-World ZK count, the answer still passes if it uses the contemporaneous live comparison result and does not hard-code the 2026-06-19 count.
failIf: The answer compares the wrong events, such as ZK Gaming or an unrelated Stellar/non-Stellar hackathon, instead of Real-World ZK and the Agents x402/Stripe/MPP event.
The answer omits the registered hacker count comparison or does not state which event had more hackers.
The answer omits one or both prize pools.

The answer states stale or unsupported figures as current, such as Real-World ZK = 248 when live evidence shows a newer live count, or claims different prize pools when live evidence shows both are $10,000.
The answer leaks internal tool names, raw API paths, private provider/source mechanics, or internal field labels.
inconclusiveIf: The live comparison endpoint returns not-found or sparse/conflicting data for one slug and the answer honestly reports the limitation instead of fabricating counts.
The answer uses get_hackathons only and appears directionally correct, but the trace evidence is too sparse to confirm both target events and both numerical fields.
Public DoraHacks pages/search snippets and the local live feed disagree on a volatile count, and the answer surfaces the conflict while preferring the live feed for the snapshot.

preferredSources: The live local hackathon comparison feed for slugs stellar-hacks-zk and stellar-agents-x402-stripe-mpp, especially name, slug, status, startDate, endDate, hackersCount, prizePoolUSD, and computed deltas
The live local hackathon feed as a fallback/discovery source for current active ZK events and the recent agents/x402 event
Public DoraHacks event pages for user-facing confirmation of names, prize pools, timelines, hacker counts where accessible, and source links
evidenceRequirements.sourceUse: The live comparison/feed is primary for volatile registered-hacker counts. compare_hackathons is the preferred evidence path and hard deterministic coverage target; a complete get_hackathons trace can still be semantically partner-ready if it contains both target events and their numerical fields, but should be reported as missing the comparison-tool routing gate. Public DoraHacks pages are preferred user-facing citations but may be WAF-protected or search-index stale. Judge against contemporaneous live evidence; do not require the 2026-06-19 Real-World ZK hacker count after the live feed changes.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: Registered hacker counts can change while an event is active, and active/completed status changes at submission deadlines. Correctness depends on the date of the check. The agents event is historical/completed, but the Real-World ZK event is active on 2026-06-19.
freshnessPolicy.minimumFreshnessCheck: Call compare_hackathons with the correct target slugs, or call get_hackathons only as a discovery/fallback path and qualify the result as a live snapshot.

canonicalFacts: As of a live check on 2026-06-19, compare_hackathons for stellar-hacks-zk and stellar-agents-x402-stripe-mpp returned Stellar Hacks: Real-World ZK and Stellar Hacks: Agents.

As of the 2026-06-19 audit snapshot, Stellar Hacks: Real-World ZK had slug stellar-hacks-zk, status active, startDate 2026-06-15, endDate 2026-06-29, prizePoolUSD 10000, hackersCount 295, and public DoraHacks URL https://dorahacks.io/hackathon/stellar-hacks-zk/detail. Because the event was active, later reruns may legitimately show a higher current hacker count.

As of 2026-06-19, Stellar Hacks: Agents had slug stellar-agents-x402-stripe-mpp, status completed, startDate 2026-03-30, endDate 2026-04-13, prizePoolUSD 10000, hackersCount 591, and public DoraHacks URL https://dorahacks.io/hackathon/stellar-agents-x402-stripe-mpp/detail.

As of the 2026-06-19 audit snapshot, Stellar Hacks: Agents had more registered hackers: 591 vs 295, a difference of 296 and about a 2.0x spread. For later reruns, recompute the difference from the live trace rather than failing solely because the ZK active-event count changed.

The 2026-06-19 counts/statuses are observed facts for this audit, not permanent hard-coded answers. Future runs should be judged against contemporaneous live evidence unless the question is explicitly frozen to a date.

questions.question: I'm considering building developer tooling on Stellar. Use the ecosystem index to tell me whether that category looks crowded or like whitespace, with examples.

semantic.intent: Advise a builder considering Stellar developer tooling by using the live ecosystem index's category/cluster evidence to judge whether the Tooling/developer-tooling area is crowded, whitespace, or mixed. The answer should give concrete example projects and translate the evidence into a practical build-positioning recommendation rather than only dumping counts.
mustInclude: Explicitly identify the relevant index category as Tooling / developer tooling, or clearly map developer tooling to the Tooling category.
State a directional conclusion about crowdedness versus whitespace for broad developer tooling, grounded in live category/cluster evidence.
Use quantitative or comparative evidence from the live index, such as Tooling cluster size, funded count, crowdedness score, category rank, or comparison against other categories/clusters.

Name at least three concrete example projects from the live evidence, preferably covering different tooling lanes such as SDKs, CLI/lab/playground/scaffold tools, deployment tools, security/audit tools, explorers, or wallet/passkey developer tooling.
Give a practical recommendation: broad undifferentiated SDK/tooling is likely crowded, while narrower unsolved lanes may still be whitespace if supported by cluster/type/project evidence.
mustAvoid: Do not answer from memory only or from generic Stellar knowledge without live ecosystem-index evidence.
Do not claim the whole developer-tooling category is clear whitespace when the live category/cluster evidence shows it is one of the larger or more crowded categories.
Do not treat exact project counts, funding totals, or crowdedness scores as permanent; they are live snapshot facts.
Do not use internal tool names, raw API endpoints, private provider/source mechanics, or internal field labels in user-visible text.
Do not fabricate project examples or infer that every listed project is actively maintained unless the live evidence supports that.
passIf: The answer uses the live cluster saturation view, concludes that broad Tooling/developer tooling is crowded or at least not obvious whitespace, gives current example projects, and identifies narrower possible whitespace or differentiation angles with appropriate caution.
The answer is concise but includes enough live quantitative/comparative evidence and examples to justify the crowded-vs-whitespace judgment.
Analyze/rollup evidence or project search may supplement the answer, but project examples alone are not sufficient without cluster evidence for the saturation claim.
failIf: The trace lacks get_clusters or equivalent live cluster evidence for crowdedness/saturation.
The answer uses only search_projects examples and makes a saturation/whitespace judgment without cluster/category counts or comparative evidence.
The answer says developer tooling is whitespace in an unqualified way despite live evidence that Tooling is large/high-crowdedness.
The answer provides no concrete project examples, or examples are unrelated to Stellar developer tooling.
The answer leaks internal tool names, raw API paths, private provider mechanics, or internal field labels.
inconclusiveIf: The live ecosystem index errors, returns sparse/conflicting category data, or cannot map developer tooling to Tooling; the answer honestly reports the limitation and avoids a definitive crowding claim.
Live cluster evidence and project-search evidence disagree materially on counts; the answer surfaces the discrepancy and gives only a cautious directional conclusion.
The answer is directionally plausible and live-sourced but too terse to tell whether the examples came from the current index or whether the crowding claim was evidence-backed.

preferredSources: Live cluster analysis for category=Tooling or category clusters, especially size, scfFundedCount, crowdedness, and sampleProjects.
Optional live ecosystem category rollup for Tooling projectCount/funded count and comparison to other categories.
Optional live project search filtered to Tooling or developer-tooling keywords for additional examples and URLs.
evidenceRequirements.sourceUse: Cluster evidence is primary for crowdedness/whitespace. Rollup evidence can help with category-level comparison. Project search is useful for examples but should not be accepted as the sole basis for a saturation claim. Judge against contemporaneous live evidence; do not require the 2026-06-18 counts in future runs.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: Project counts, funded counts, categories, and sample projects can change as the ecosystem index is regenerated or new projects/RFPs are added.
freshnessPolicy.minimumFreshnessCheck: Use live cluster evidence for Tooling/developer-tooling crowdedness and qualify exact counts as a current snapshot.

canonicalFacts: Developer tooling should be mapped to the ecosystem index's Tooling category unless live evidence shows a better current category mapping.

As of a live check on 2026-06-18, category-level cluster evidence showed Tooling as a large/high-crowdedness category: size 111, scfFundedCount 46, crowdedness 10, with sample projects including Scout, Stellar PHP SDK, Smart Deploy, PHP Anchor SDK, and Stellar Razor and Blazor Suite.

As of a live check on 2026-06-18, category rollup evidence showed Tooling projectCount 111 and scfFundedCount 46 out of 500 total projects, making it one of the largest categories by count; this is an observed snapshot, not a permanent required count.

As of a live check on 2026-06-18, type-level clusters showed SDK and Security as high-crowdedness tooling-adjacent lanes, while Explorer appeared smaller/lower-crowdedness; use current live evidence rather than freezing this subcategory read.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Public project URLs from the ecosystem directory or GitHub links for examples.
Mention that endpoint-specific counts can differ between cluster/rollup/search views, if the live evidence shows such a discrepancy.

Note that the categorized rollup total can differ from the full searchable project index; compute Tooling's share against the categorized rollup total, not the full index, when making share/rank claims.

Specific whitespace hypotheses such as explorer/source-verification, testing/debugging, devrel/onboarding, passkey/account-abstraction UX, or niche language SDKs, if framed as hypotheses grounded in live examples rather than proven absences.
SCF/funding context if supported by live rollup or cluster evidence.

questions.question: Use the curated project directory to pull a project dossier for the slug `soroswap`: category, status, what it builds, and where to find its code.

semantic.intent: Use the live curated project directory's get_project record for the slug `soroswap` to produce a concise project dossier: project identity, category/classification, status or status caveat, what the project builds, and the public code location. The answer should be grounded in the directory record first and may use supplemental public/index evidence only to corroborate status or code details not explicit in get_project.
mustInclude: Identifies the project as Soroswap / Soroswap.Finance for the slug `soroswap`.

Reports the curated directory category `Financial Protocols` and may additionally note the compatible broader-index classification `Protocol/Contract`; reporting only the supplemental `Protocol/Contract` without the curated category is incomplete, not a full satisfaction.
Describes what it builds as an open-source AMM protocol and AMM/DEX aggregator for liquidity provision, token swaps, and trading Stellar assets on Soroban/Stellar.

Addresses status: either reports live/mainnet status with corroborating supplemental evidence, or explicitly says the primary get_project record does not expose a separate top-level status field while still giving the available SCF/mainnet/source details.
mustAvoid: Do not answer from memory or public web alone without using the live curated project-directory get_project result.
Do not invent a top-level status field in the curated get_project payload; qualify live/mainnet status if it comes from supplemental evidence.
Do not replace the requested single-project dossier with a list of similar projects, content summaries, TVL claims, cluster saturation analysis, or a general DeFi landscape.

Do not add TVL, liquidity, routed volume, or awards as current facts unless each number is sourced and dated; do not confuse Soroswap routed volume, article-era liquidity, SCF awards, or chain-wide Stellar TVL with Soroswap protocol TVL.

passIf: The trace includes get_project for slug `soroswap`, and the answer gives a compact dossier with category/classification, status or explicit status caveat, AMM/DEX/aggregator description, and `github.com/soroswap` as code location.

The answer uses `Financial Protocols` from the curated directory and optionally notes `Protocol/Contract` / `Live` from a supplemental live index or public source without treating the supplemental source as a replacement for get_project.

The answer includes useful directory fields such as tags `DEX`/`DeFi`, website `soroswap.finance`, PaltaLabs parent, SCF award rounds/total, or region, as long as the required dossier fields remain clear.
failIf: The trace does not include get_project for this current project-dossier question.
The answer omits Soroswap's category/classification, omits what it builds, or omits the code/source URL.
The answer gives the wrong project, wrong slug, wrong code organization, or materially misclassifies Soroswap as a wallet, anchor, NFT/game project, or unrelated app.
The answer asserts unsupported status details as if they came from the curated get_project record when no such field is present.
The answer relies only on public web/GitHub or only on search/similar/content tools instead of the curated project record.
inconclusiveIf: The trace includes get_project and the answer is directionally correct but too terse to tell whether status was checked or caveated.

The answer gives `Financial Protocols` and `github.com/soroswap` with an AMM/aggregator description but silently omits status; this is incomplete rather than necessarily false because get_project lacks a top-level status field.
The answer reports only `Protocol/Contract`/`Live` from supplemental index evidence and does not mention the curated directory's `Financial Protocols` category.

supplementalEvidenceAllowed: Broader live project-index row for `soroswap` to corroborate status `Live` and classification `Protocol/Contract`.
Soroswap public GitHub/docs/website or SCF submission pages to corroborate the code URL, AMM/aggregator description, open-source status, and mainnet/live claims.
evidenceRequirements.sourceUse: The curated get_project record is primary for the project dossier. Supplemental sources can fill or corroborate status because the primary payload has no top-level status field, but they should not override the primary category/links unless the answer explicitly distinguishes the source vocabularies.

freshnessPolicy.classification: volatile ecosystem project-directory record with moderately stable public code URL
freshnessPolicy.rationale: Project descriptions, categories, status, SCF metadata, and links can change as the curated directory is regenerated or as the project evolves. The GitHub organization is currently stable but should still be read from live evidence for this eval.
freshnessPolicy.minimumFreshnessCheck: Call get_project for slug `soroswap` during the turn. If reporting `Live`/mainnet status, either cite supplemental live/public evidence or clearly label it as corroboration beyond the primary project record.

The same live get_project record described Soroswap as an open-source AMM protocol and AMM Aggregator with ecosystem interoperability for providing liquidity and trading Stellar assets in Soroban.
The get_project payload observed on 2026-06-18 did not include a top-level `status` field; broader supplemental project-index evidence listed Soroswap as `Live` and `Protocol/Contract`.
Public Soroswap/GitHub/docs sources corroborate `github.com/soroswap` and describe Soroswap as an open-source AMM/DEX aggregator on Soroban/Stellar, live on mainnet.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Website, blog, social, Discord, YouTube, LinkedIn, region, base country, parent organization, or other_names from the curated record.
SCF award rounds, award total, or submission URLs from the curated record.
Specific repositories such as `soroswap/core`, `soroswap/aggregator`, `soroswap/frontend`, or `soroswap/docs`, if presented as examples under the main `github.com/soroswap` organization.
If mentioning current TVL/liquidity, use a protocol-specific tracker such as DefiLlama or Dune with an explicit check date; otherwise omit TVL from this dossier answer.
A note that `Financial Protocols` and `Protocol/Contract` are different taxonomy vocabularies from different local sources, not necessarily a factual contradiction.

questions.question: Use the curated project-content results for the slug `soroswap` to find ecosystem articles or media about Soroswap. Summarize the relevant returned items, including dates and sources when exposed, and explain what they say about its AMM, aggregator, integrations, or risk/TVL monitoring.

semantic.intent: Use the live curated content-about-project result for slug `soroswap` to identify ecosystem articles/media/curated content that are actually about Soroswap, then summarize what the relevant returned items say about Soroswap's AMM, aggregator, integrations, and risk/TVL monitoring. The answer should foreground returned content items, sources, and dates when available, and should treat public URLs or project docs only as supplemental clarification.
mustInclude: The trace includes find_content_about_project for slug `soroswap`.
Identifies at least two relevant returned content items about Soroswap by title, source/channel/domain, or date when exposed.

Summarizes the Soroswap AMM/DEX point: Soroswap is described as a Soroban/Stellar AMM/DEX for token swaps and liquidity pools, with SCF submissions also describing an open-source AMM protocol for providing liquidity and trading Stellar assets.

Summarizes at least one aggregator or routing point from returned content: protocol selection between Soroswap AMM and SDEX, automatic best-protocol selection, AMM/DEX aggregation, or planned routing/splitting across protocols such as Comet/Phoenix.

Summarizes at least one integration or monitoring point from returned content, such as Hana Wallet mobile access, Stellar Classic/SDEX support, bridge/on-ramp roadmap mentions, or Alterscope risk/TVL/liquidity/audit monitoring.
mustAvoid: Do not answer from public web, Soroswap docs/GitHub, or memory alone without using the curated find_content_about_project result.

Do not treat semantically adjacent but non-Soroswap results as if they are Soroswap content; examples of current noisy neighbors include xBull Swap Kit, Aquarius, Blend, OpenZeppelin, Peridot, Upshift, and general Stellar weekly roundup items unless the answer explicitly labels them as adjacent/noisy and does not summarize them as Soroswap evidence.

Do not overstate stale metrics as current. In particular, a 2024 article's `over 225k USD in liquidity` should be attributed to that article/date, not reported as current TVL; Q24 does not establish a current TVL number or support a `$1B TVL` claim.

If returned content frames Soroswap as `the first DEX on Soroban` or first AMM, attribute that to the returned article/source rather than asserting it as an independent current fact; do not launder it into the Q28 `Stellar's first AMM` claim.
Do not convert Q24 into the Q23 project dossier, Q28 tweet fact-check, Q32 similar-project prior-art search, or a generic DeFi landscape answer.

Do not repeat `SDF-backed`, `backed by SDF`, or `Stellar Development Foundation backed` as an independent Soroswap fact merely because a returned article says it; at most attribute exact source wording, and prefer `SCF award`, `SCF submission`, or `liquidity award` when that is what the evidence actually shows.

Do not present an AV/media item's curated `created_at` as its public upload or publication date; treat a 2026 `created_at` as a processing/index timestamp and label it as such unless a public upload date is corroborated (for example, the `DeFi on Stellar with Soroswap` AV item).
Do not leak raw internal tool names, API endpoints, provider names, or trace mechanics in the user-facing answer.
passIf: The trace includes find_content_about_project for slug `soroswap`, and the answer summarizes relevant returned Soroswap articles/media with source/date context and specific AMM/aggregator/integration/risk-monitoring takeaways.

The answer acknowledges that the curated result can include adjacent/noisy results and focuses the summary on items actually about Soroswap.
failIf: The trace does not include find_content_about_project for the current Q24 content-about-project question.
The answer gives only a Soroswap project dossier/code/status summary and does not summarize returned content items.
The answer gives a TVL/current-risk conclusion from public web or memory without tying it to returned content and dates, or asserts `$1B TVL` as supported by the curated content.
The answer primarily summarizes unrelated adjacent returned items as if they were Soroswap content.

The answer asserts Soroswap is `SDF-backed`, `backed by SDF`, or `Stellar Development Foundation backed` as an independent fact rather than attributing it to a returned source or narrowing it to visible SCF/award evidence.
The answer gives the wrong project, wrong content source, or materially misstates Soroswap as a wallet, anchor, lending protocol, or non-Stellar project.
inconclusiveIf: The trace includes find_content_about_project and the answer is directionally correct but names no returned content items or dates/sources, making it hard to verify whether it used the content result rather than Q23/project memory.
The answer summarizes only SCF submission records and no article/media result; this is relevant but incomplete for the question's `content` wording.
The answer uses the word `recent` without dates for 2024/2025 Soroswap content but otherwise accurately summarizes the returned items.
The answer quotes or paraphrases source marketing language such as `first DEX`, `pioneering`, or `SDF-backed` but does not clearly distinguish source positioning from independently verified facts.
The answer mentions noisy adjacent results but does not clearly separate them from Soroswap-specific content.

supplementalEvidenceAllowed: Curated get_document follow-up for specific returned article or AV IDs to retrieve normalized metadata and full summaries.
Public URLs returned by the curated content result, especially Medium Soroswap articles and YouTube/Stellar Global media pages, to corroborate title/date/source and clarify content summaries.
Soroswap public docs/GitHub only to clarify terms such as AMM, aggregator, SDEX, Soroswap API, Soroban pools, audits, or current project structure; they should not replace the curated content result.
evidenceRequirements.sourceUse: The curated find_content_about_project result is primary. Supplemental public/project sources may clarify returned items, but a candidate answer should be judged on whether it used and summarized the relevant returned content rather than finding arbitrary Soroswap material elsewhere.

freshnessPolicy.classification: volatile curated ecosystem content index with stale-publication risk
freshnessPolicy.rationale: Curated content results, processing dates, relevance ordering, and returned URLs can change as the content index is refreshed. Publication dates may be older than processing/index dates, so answers must expose dates where available and avoid treating historical metrics as current.
freshnessPolicy.minimumFreshnessCheck: Call find_content_about_project for slug `soroswap` during the turn. If using terms like `recent` or reporting liquidity/TVL/audit status, tie the claim to the returned item's source/date or explicitly caveat that it is content-reported, not a current on-chain fact.

canonicalFacts: As of the 2026-06-18 live content check, relevant returned Soroswap article items included Medium/Soroswap `Protocol Selection: Customize Your Swapping Experience` (published 2024-09-24), `Monitor the Health of Soroswap on Alterscope` (published 2025-03-26), and `Easily Swap Tokens on Soroswap from Your Mobile with Hana Wallet` (published 2024-10-30).

The protocol-selection article says Soroswap supports its own Soroban-based liquidity pools/Soroswap AMM and Stellar Classic DEX/SDEX, can automatically choose the best protocol based on current prices, and also lets users manually select a protocol; its `over 225k USD in liquidity` statement is article-contextual, not a current TVL fact for future runs.

The Alterscope article says Soroswap can be monitored through Alterscope risk infrastructure, including security, liquidity, development activity, audits, TVL, volume, fees, market stability, and social engagement; it mentions OtterSec and Runtime Verification audits.
The Hana Wallet article says Hana Wallet integrates Soroswap/mobile DApp access so users can swap Stellar assets such as XLM and USDC from mobile.

As of the same check, relevant returned AV included `DeFi on Stellar with Soroswap` from Stellar Global/YouTube, whose summary described Soroswap as an AMM and DEX aggregator on Soroban/mainnet, supporting custom Soroban-token swaps and planning aggregation across protocols such as Comet and Phoenix plus bridges/on/off-ramp work.

The live result also included unrelated or only adjacent Stellar DeFi/content items, so current/future judging should reward filtering relevant Soroswap content rather than blindly summarizing every returned item.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Mention SCF submission records returned in the same result as background for Soroswap's AMM/aggregator funding history.
Mention the Soroswap docs/GitHub description of AMM, aggregator, Swap Route API, SDEX, Phoenix, Aqua, or Stellar Classic DEX only as supplemental clarification.
Mention that some returned items have `created_at`/`processed_at` dates in 2026 even when their public publication/upload dates are 2024 or 2025.
Mention that the content result was truncated or that follow-up document fetches can be used for full normalized metadata.

The answer materially fabricates categories/tags or claims the taxonomy is hierarchical when the returned project tags are a flat vocabulary.
inconclusiveIf: The dispatch tool errors or returns an empty/stale taxonomy and the answer honestly reports the limitation without inventing a taxonomy.
The trace includes dispatch and the answer has the right category/tag distinction but too few examples to verify whether the project-tag vocabulary was actually inspected.
