Each trustline is an account entry that raises the account's minimum XLM balance by the base reserve (about 0.5 XLM per trustline, indicative/parameter-dependent), so the account must first be funded/activated with XLM.

You add a trustline from a wallet (Freighter, LOBSTR) via 'Manage assets': search the asset, confirm the correct issuer (e.g. Circle for USDC), add it, and sign; DEXs/aggregators (Soroswap, Phoenix, Decaf) can also create it, sometimes bundled into a swap.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Note that a trustline can later be removed (set limit to 0) to reclaim the reserve once the balance is zero.
Warning about scam/fake assets sharing a legitimate asset code, reinforcing issuer verification.
Mention that the trustline can carry a limit on how much of the asset the account will hold.
niceToHave: Names a specific real issuer (Circle for USDC) so the user knows what to verify.
Distinguishes the manual 'Manage assets' flow from the swap-bundled trustline creation on a DEX.

questions.question: How do Stellar liquidity pools / AMMs work, and how do you provide liquidity (why two assets, where do the fees go)?

answerRegex: two assets|both assets|asset pair|trading pair|deposit both

semantic.intent: Explain how a Stellar liquidity pool / AMM works as a constant-product (Protocol 18) automated market maker, why a pool holds two assets, how a user provides liquidity (deposit both assets for pool shares, redeem to withdraw), and where swap fees accrue. Give an accurate conceptual + how-to picture, not a code template.
mustInclude: An AMM/liquidity pool replaces an orderbook counterparty and auto-prices swaps from the ratio of its two reserves using a constant-product invariant (x·y = k), introduced natively in Protocol 18.
A pool must hold BOTH assets of the pair because the price of one asset is expressed relative to the other; larger reserves / smaller trades mean less price movement (slippage).
You provide liquidity by depositing both assets of the pair in return for pool shares, and you redeem those shares to withdraw your assets proportional to your ownership.

Each swap charges a fixed 0.3% fee that is added back into the pool's reserves (so the constant product grows); LPs realize fees as the ability to withdraw more value than they deposited, and are exposed to impermanent loss when the two assets' relative prices diverge.
mustAvoid: Claiming a single-asset deposit is the normal way to provide liquidity to a standard Stellar AMM pool (it is a two-asset, balanced deposit).
Saying swap fees are paid out separately or to the protocol rather than accruing into the pool's reserves for LPs.
Asserting LPs are immune to loss / that impermanent loss does not exist.
passIf: The answer frames the pool as a two-asset constant-product AMM, describes depositing both assets for pool shares and redeeming to withdraw, and correctly says the 0.3% swap fee accrues into the pool reserves for LPs.
It conveys the constant-product pricing/slippage idea and that fees grow the pool (with impermanent-loss exposure), even without exact formulas.
failIf: It says fees are paid out to LPs separately or go to the protocol/network rather than back into the pool's reserves.
It claims you provide liquidity with a single asset, or omits that a pool holds two assets.
It states LPs face no risk / ignores or denies impermanent loss.
inconclusiveIf: It mentions liquidity pools/AMMs generically but omits the key pillar of where fees accrue (added to reserves) or that you deposit both assets for pool shares.
It gestures at fees and pricing but never connects them to the constant-product reserves or LP withdrawal.
canonicalFacts: A Stellar liquidity pool/AMM holds reserves of a two-asset trading pair and replaces an orderbook counterparty, auto-pricing swaps from the reserve ratio via a constant-product invariant (L = X·Y, the Uniswap model), introduced natively in Protocol 18.
It needs both assets because the price of asset A is quoted relative to asset B; larger reserves and smaller trades mean less slippage.
You deposit BOTH assets in return for pool shares and redeem those shares to withdraw, proportional to your pool-share ownership.

Each swap charges a fixed 0.3% fee (F = 0.003) that is added to the pool's reserves rather than paid out, so the constant product grows over time and LPs realize fees as the chance to withdraw more value than they deposited.
LPs are exposed to impermanent loss when the relative prices of the two assets diverge.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: Stellar AMM mechanics (constant-product pricing, two-asset deposits for pool shares, the fixed 0.3% fee, impermanent loss) are stable protocol facts since Protocol 18; no live data is needed to explain them.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the assets/DeFi skill as a stable protocol fact; no point-in-time figures required.
The 0.3% fee and constant-product invariant are fixed Protocol 18 AMM parameters, not market-dependent.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Mention of path payments / SDEX interplay where AMM pools and the orderbook are both routed.
Concrete CLI/SDK operation names (liquidity pool deposit / withdraw) or pool-share ID details.
A worked numeric slippage or impermanent-loss example.
niceToHave: Distinguishes slippage (trade-time price impact) from impermanent loss (LP exposure to divergence).
Notes the deposit is balanced to the current reserve ratio.

questions.question: How does Stellar handle spam, scam tokens, and fraudulent claimable balances on an open network?

semantic.intent: Explain that Stellar is an open/permissionless network where anyone can issue an asset, so spam and scam protection comes from protocol design and user diligence rather than a central gatekeeper, and tell the user concretely how to protect themselves from unsolicited tokens and fraudulent claimable balances.
mustInclude: Stellar is open/permissionless: anyone can issue an asset/token, so there is no central authority that pre-vets or blocks tokens.

You cannot be force-fed an asset — holding any non-native asset requires opting in via an explicit trustline (which locks a base reserve), and unsolicited claimable balances are the main spam/phishing vector and can simply be ignored or declined.

The Stellar Development Foundation does not control the network and cannot unilaterally freeze, seize, or return tokens/XLM; users must self-protect by verifying the issuer/domain and watching for red flags like assets with few funded trustlines or look-alike/false-claim tokens.

Active fraud/theft response happens at the validator layer — Tier-1 validators can coordinate to reject attacker transactions — and SDF is formalizing this into a protocol-native quorum-freeze mechanism (CAP-77).
mustAvoid: Claiming the Stellar Development Foundation (or any central operator) can freeze, blacklist, or reverse arbitrary user tokens/XLM at will.
Implying spam/scam tokens can land in and affect your account without any opt-in, or that accepting/claiming an unsolicited claimable balance is safe by default.
Stating Stellar pre-screens or whitelists token issuers so scam tokens cannot exist.
passIf: The answer establishes the open/permissionless model, makes clear you opt in via trustlines and can ignore/decline unsolicited claimable balances, and frames defense as user diligence plus validator-layer response (not a central gatekeeper that vets tokens).
failIf: It says SDF or some central authority vets/whitelists tokens or can unilaterally freeze/seize/reverse user funds at will.
It claims you can be force-fed an asset with no opt-in, or advises claiming/trusting unsolicited claimable balances.
It frames Stellar as having no scam-mitigation at all and gives no protective guidance (trustlines, ignoring claimable balances, issuer verification).
inconclusiveIf: It mentions scams/spam generally but omits the key pillars — the trustline opt-in / declinable claimable balances and the no-central-gatekeeper framing.
It gives protective tips but mischaracterizes who can intervene (vague on whether any party can freeze funds).
canonicalFacts: Stellar is an open, permissionless network: anyone can issue an asset/token, so there is no central party that pre-vets or blocks tokens.
Holding any non-native asset requires opting in via an explicit trustline, which locks a base reserve (indicative: ~0.5 XLM, subject to network reserve settings); you cannot be force-fed an asset.
Unsolicited claimable balances are the main spam/phishing vector (SDF likens them to email spam); you can simply ignore or decline ones you don't trust.

The Stellar Development Foundation does not control the network and cannot unilaterally freeze, seize, or return tokens/XLM; users self-protect via issuer/domain verification and red-flag awareness (few funded trustlines, look-alike or false-claim tokens).

Active fraud response is validator-driven: Tier-1 validators can coordinate to reject attacker transactions (e.g., the Feb 2026 ~$10M Blend oracle-manipulation attack, where ~73% / ~48M XLM was quarantined), and SDF's proposed CAP-77 "Quorum Freeze" formalizes this into a consensus-driven, reversible, auditable onchain freeze.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The core protocol design (permissionless issuance, trustline opt-in, declinable claimable balances, no central freeze authority) is stable; the validator-coordination precedent and CAP-77 are durable named facts, not point-in-time figures.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the assets skill / stable protocol facts (trustline opt-in, claimable-balance semantics, permissionless issuance).

The validator-layer freeze precedent and CAP-77 are named ecosystem facts (SDF anti-scam guidance, Blockaid '73% Quarantined' report, SDF Quorum Freeze proposal); exact amounts/percentages are indicative.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Mention of issuer authorization flags / regulated-asset controls (authorization required, clawback) as issuer-level tools distinct from network-wide control.
Concrete phishing red flags or steps to remove/clean up an unwanted trustline.
Note that wallets/explorers often hide or flag unsolicited claimable balances and spam assets.
niceToHave: Distinguishes issuer-level controls (clawback / auth flags on a specific asset) from network-level intervention (validator quorum / CAP-77).
Frames CAP-77 quorum freeze as reversible and auditable, contrasting it with arbitrary central seizure.

questions.question: How do I fund/activate a new Stellar account, what is the minimum XLM balance requirement, and does an account exist before it is funded?

semantic.intent: Explain that a Stellar account does not exist on the ledger until it is funded with the required minimum reserve, state that minimum and how it is derived, and describe how a new account is created/activated (Create Account from a funded account, or Friendbot on testnet).
mustInclude: Generating a keypair only produces an address; the account does not exist on the ledger until it is funded with the minimum balance.
The base minimum is 1 XLM, derived from 2 base entries times the 0.5 XLM base reserve per entry.
A new account is created/activated via the Create Account operation from an existing funded account (specifying a starting balance), or via Friendbot on testnet.
Each additional ledger entry (trustline, signer, offer, data entry) raises the minimum reserve by another 0.5 XLM.
mustAvoid: Claiming a keypair/account already exists on the ledger before it is funded.
Stating the minimum balance as something other than 1 XLM (e.g. 20 XLM, or 0.5 XLM) or treating the 0.5 XLM base reserve as the account minimum.
Saying you fund a brand-new account with a normal Payment operation rather than Create Account.
passIf: The answer says the account does not exist until funded, gives the 1 XLM minimum (and ideally the 2 entries x 0.5 XLM derivation), and names Create Account (or Friendbot on testnet) as the activation path.
failIf: It says the account exists on the ledger as soon as you generate a keypair (before funding).
It gives a wrong minimum (not 1 XLM) or misattributes the base reserve.
It tells the user to activate a new account with a Payment operation instead of Create Account.
inconclusiveIf: It mentions a minimum balance or funding but omits the 1 XLM figure / its derivation, or never states that the account doesn't exist until funded.
It describes account creation but is vague about the actual minimum reserve.
canonicalFacts: A Stellar account does not exist on the ledger until funded; a keypair alone only yields an address.
The minimum balance is 1 XLM = 2 base entries x 0.5 XLM base reserve per entry.
Each additional entry (trustline, signer, offer, data entry) adds 0.5 XLM to the minimum reserve.
A new account is created via the Create Account operation from an existing funded account (with a starting balance), or via Friendbot on testnet.
Under CAP-33 (Protocol 15) another account can sponsor the reserves, allowing the new account's own starting balance to be zero.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The account-existence model, the 1 XLM minimum, the 0.5 XLM base reserve, and the Create Account / Friendbot activation paths are stable protocol facts that do not change with live network state.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the data skill / stable Stellar protocol facts; grounded in SDF talks in the LumenLoop AV corpus (av 1315, av 1407).
allowedExtraNotRequired: Mentioning CAP-33 (Protocol 15) reserve sponsorship enabling a zero starting balance.
Noting that the base reserve is a network parameter set by validators (currently 0.5 XLM).
Pointing out that dropping below the minimum reserve prevents spending those reserved lumens.
niceToHave: Distinguishing testnet activation (Friendbot) from mainnet (Create Account from a funded account).
Framing the minimum as scaling with the number of subentries the account holds.

questions.question: Is the LOBSTR wallet legitimate and safe, and where is its recovery phrase / secret stored?

semantic.intent: Reassure a retail user that LOBSTR is a legitimate, well-established Stellar wallet and accurately explain its custody model: by default it is non-custodial / self-custody, with the recovery phrase and keys generated and held on the user's own device, plus an optional opt-in step that stores the phrase with LOBSTR to enable the multi-wallet feature.
mustInclude: Confirms LOBSTR is a legitimate, long-established Stellar wallet (built by Ultra Stellar, the team behind StellarX, StellarTerm, and LOBSTR Vault; an SCF-awarded project).
States the default model is non-custodial / self-custody: keys are generated from and stored on the user's own device, so the user controls the funds.
Explains the recovery phrase is normally NOT stored by LOBSTR unless the user performs the optional one-time opt-in to 'connect' the recovery phrase (which unlocks multi-wallet).
Frames the recovery phrase as the user's own responsibility to back up securely and never share.
mustAvoid: Calling LOBSTR a scam, untrustworthy, or unverified.
Claiming LOBSTR is custodial by default or that it always holds your recovery phrase/keys.
Telling the user it is safe to share or upload their recovery phrase to anyone.
passIf: The answer affirms LOBSTR is legitimate/established AND correctly states it is non-custodial by default with on-device key/phrase storage, noting the optional opt-in step stores the phrase with LOBSTR for multi-wallet.
failIf: It says LOBSTR is illegitimate/a scam, or that it routinely stores your recovery phrase/keys by default.
It mischaracterizes the custody model as custodial, or implies LOBSTR holds your keys even without the opt-in step.
It encourages sharing the recovery phrase or treats it as not security-critical.
inconclusiveIf: It vaguely calls LOBSTR safe without addressing where the recovery phrase/keys are stored.
It describes self-custody but omits or muddles the optional opt-in phrase-storage / multi-wallet distinction.
canonicalFacts: LOBSTR is a legitimate, long-established non-custodial Stellar wallet built by Ultra Stellar (the company behind StellarX, StellarTerm, and LOBSTR Vault).
It is an SCF-awarded project (SCF #2, #17, #22; dollar figures indicative and historical).
By default LOBSTR uses on-device key storage: a Recovery Phrase is generated and the keys are held on the user's device (self-custody).

Users can optionally perform a one-time step to 'connect' their Recovery Phrase to their LOBSTR account, which securely stores the phrase with LOBSTR to enable the multi-wallet feature; absent that, LOBSTR does not store the phrase/secret.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: Legitimacy and the broad custody model are stable, but trust questions benefit from current corpus/directory confirmation of the project and any recent custody-feature changes; SCF award figures should be cited from the corpus rather than memory.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the stellar-project-dossier skill / LumenLoop directory (ultra-stellar project) and the LOBSTR official support docs in the corpus (article id 2438).
allowedExtraNotRequired: Mention of LOBSTR Vault as a separate hardware/cold-storage offering.
Specific SCF award amounts as indicative figures.
General self-custody safety tips (write down the phrase offline, beware phishing).
niceToHave: Clarifies WHY the opt-in phrase storage exists (to enable multi-wallet across devices) so the user understands the tradeoff.
Notes that backing up and securing the recovery phrase is what ultimately protects the funds, regardless of the wallet's reputation.

questions.question: How do I move XLM out of a custodial exchange (Coinbase, Uphold, Kraken, etc.) into a non-custodial or hardware wallet?

semantic.intent: Walk a retail user through withdrawing XLM from a custodial exchange (Coinbase, Uphold, Kraken, etc.) into self-custody: set up a non-custodial Stellar wallet, get its public (G-prefixed) address, and Send/Withdraw to it, while flagging the Stellar-specific gotchas (memo, base reserve/activation, trustline) and the custody trade-off.
mustInclude: Set up a non-custodial Stellar wallet (software like Freighter, LOBSTR, or xBull, or a hardware wallet like Ledger/Trezor), then use its public Stellar address (starts with G) as the destination when you Send/Withdraw XLM on the exchange.
A destination memo/tag is only needed when withdrawing to a shared/pooled exchange deposit address; for your own personal wallet address you normally leave the memo blank.

A brand-new Stellar account must be funded enough to meet the network base reserve (~1 XLM minimum balance, indicative) to activate, and you must add a trustline before holding non-XLM assets like USDC.
With a non-custodial/hardware wallet you alone hold the private keys and seed/recovery phrase, so back them up offline; no one can move or recover your funds without your signature.
mustAvoid: Telling the user to always attach a memo when sending to their own personal wallet address (memos are only for shared/pooled exchange deposit addresses).
Implying a fresh Stellar account is usable with zero balance, i.e. omitting that it must be funded past the base reserve to activate.
Suggesting an exchange or third party can recover funds from a self-custody wallet if the seed phrase is lost.
passIf: The answer covers the core flow (create a non-custodial wallet, use its G-address as the Send/Withdraw destination) and surfaces at least the key Stellar gotchas (memo only for pooled addresses, base-reserve activation/trustline) plus the self-custody key-ownership trade-off.
failIf: It says you must always include a memo when withdrawing to your own personal wallet.
It omits or contradicts the base-reserve activation requirement (e.g. claims a new account works with no XLM).
It mischaracterizes custody, e.g. claims the exchange or wallet provider can recover your funds if you lose the seed phrase.
inconclusiveIf: It gives only generic 'create a wallet and withdraw' steps without any of the Stellar-specific gotchas (memo handling, base reserve/activation, trustline) or the custody trade-off.
It names a wallet and the G-address step but never addresses whether a memo is needed.
canonicalFacts: Self-custody flow: create a non-custodial Stellar wallet (software: Freighter, LOBSTR, xBull, Lumexo, Stellarport, Interstellar; hardware: Ledger, Trezor, OneKey, MXlet), copy its public address (starts with 'G'), then on the exchange choose Send/Withdraw XLM and paste the G-address as the destination.
A destination memo/tag is only required when sending to a shared/pooled exchange deposit address; for your own personal wallet address the memo is normally left blank.

A brand-new Stellar account must receive enough XLM to meet the network base reserve (~1 XLM minimum balance, indicative/varies) before it activates; holding non-XLM assets like USDC additionally requires adding a trustline.

With a non-custodial/hardware wallet you alone hold the private keys and seed/recovery phrase: nobody can move your funds without your signature, and nobody can recover them for you if the seed is lost.
XLM transfers settle on-chain in a few seconds for a fraction-of-a-cent network fee, and the sending wallet signs/authorizes the transaction.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The withdrawal flow, memo semantics, base-reserve activation, trustline requirement, and custody trade-off are stable protocol/UX facts; only the specific wallet roster and the exact base-reserve figure drift slowly and are indicative.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from stable Stellar protocol facts (base reserve, trustlines, memo semantics) and the wallet/ecosystem references; specific wallet names and the ~1 XLM reserve figure are indicative and may change.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Naming additional wallets (Lumexo, Stellarport, Interstellar, OneKey, MXlet) or noting Freighter is the SDF-built browser extension.
Mentioning the fast settlement and fraction-of-a-cent network fee.
Advising the user to send a small test amount first.
niceToHave: Explains why pooled exchange addresses need a memo (to route to the right user) versus personal addresses not needing one.
Notes that the base-reserve XLM remains locked as minimum balance and cannot be fully drained.

questions.question: What are the notable real-world projects built on Stellar that have actual utility and can be used today?

semantic.intent: Give a retail user a concrete, current list of real, in-production Stellar projects with genuine utility they can use today, grouped by what they actually do (payments, tokenized real-world assets, DeFi), rather than abstract chain advantages or aspirational roadmap items.
mustInclude: Names live payments/remittance utility — e.g. MoneyGram's crypto-to-cash network on Stellar and/or natively issued USDC as a usable stablecoin (MGUSD may also be cited).
Cites tokenized real-world assets in production, e.g. Spiko's tokenized money-market / Treasury (EU T-Bill / US Treasury) funds.
Lists at least one live, in-use DeFi protocol with real users/capital (e.g. Blend lending, Aquarius/AQUA, Soroswap, or DeFindex).
Frames these as working/usable today (not vaporware or future plans) and stays grounded in current ecosystem data rather than exact point-in-time figures.
mustAvoid: Presenting aspirational/roadmap or testnet-only projects as live, usable-today products.
Answering only with generic chain advantages (fast, cheap, scalable) and no named, in-production projects.
Inventing project names or attributing products to Stellar that don't run on it.
passIf: The answer names specific in-production Stellar projects spanning at least two of the three categories (payments/stablecoins, tokenized RWAs, DeFi), with at least one concrete payments name (MoneyGram/USDC) and one concrete DeFi or RWA name, framed as usable today.
It stays grounded in current ecosystem data and avoids treating any cited project as a future plan.
failIf: It gives no concrete, named, real project — only abstract chain properties or marketing.
It misclassifies roadmap/vaporware/testnet items as live usable products, or fabricates a project.
It claims Stellar lacks real-world usable apps / production DeFi.
inconclusiveIf: It names projects but doesn't establish that they are live and usable today, or covers only one category (e.g. DeFi only) while ignoring the payments pillar that defines Stellar's real-world utility.
It mentions utility vaguely without naming any specific project.
canonicalFacts: Payments/remittances: MoneyGram runs a live global crypto-to-cash network on Stellar; it launched its own MGUSD stablecoin (June 2026) to power that network; USDC is natively issued on Stellar.
Tokenized real-world assets: Spiko's tokenized money-market funds (EU T-Bill / US Treasury) are in production on Stellar and crossed ~$1B tokenized (June 2026 milestone; figure indicative/varies).

Live DeFi: Blend (lending), Aquarius/AQUA (DEX), Soroswap, and DeFindex are production-grade protocols with real users and capital (TVL figures indicative/varies — e.g. Blend >$80M, Aquarius >$40M early 2026).
These cluster into three usable-today areas: payments/stablecoins, tokenized RWAs, and DeFi.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: Which projects are live and have real traction shifts over time (new launches like MGUSD, RWA milestones, TVL movement), so the answer should be grounded in current corpus data rather than static memory; exact figures are time-varying and only indicative.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the ecosystem digest/scout skill over current corpus data (stellar.org 'What the DeFi is happening on Stellar?' corpus article 2734, MoneyGram MGUSD coverage, Spiko $1B milestone).
Cite the corpus item's date when stating traction/figures; specific TVL and tokenized totals are indicative and time-varying.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Brief one-line description of what each named project does or how a user accesses it.
Mention of additional live projects (anchors, wallets like Lobstr/Freighter) or SEP-based on/off-ramps.
Light risk framing for DeFi (smart-contract risk, TVL not endorsement).
niceToHave: Groups the projects by category (payments / RWAs / DeFi) for scannability.
Distinguishes flagship real-world adoption (MoneyGram, Spiko) from earlier-stage but live DeFi.

questions.question: Why are Stellar transaction fees so low, and what is the purpose and implications of such low fees?

answerRegex: stroop|0\.00001|base fee|minimum fee|per operation|fraction of a cent|low

semantic.intent: Explain that Stellar's low transaction fees are an intentional design choice whose purpose is spam/denial-of-service prevention rather than revenue generation, describe the small per-operation base fee, and convey the practical implications (cheap micropayments, cross-border/remittance, agent payments) plus the dynamic surge-pricing safety valve when ledgers are full.
mustInclude: Fees are deliberately low and exist to deter spam / denial-of-service abuse, not to generate revenue for validators or the SDF.
There is a small base/minimum fee charged per operation (100 stroops = 0.00001 XLM per operation; 1 stroop = 0.0000001 XLM).
Fees are not paid out to validators or the Foundation (they are burned / removed), so there is no profit incentive to raise them.
Low, predictable fees make Stellar viable for micropayments, cross-border/remittance, and agent payments, while dynamic surge pricing lets transactions bid higher fees when a ledger is full.
mustAvoid: Claiming Stellar fees are paid out to validators or the SDF as revenue/rewards.
Asserting fees are fixed/static with no mechanism to rise (ignoring dynamic surge pricing).
Framing low fees as purely a marketing perk with no anti-spam / DoS purpose.
passIf: The answer states fees are intentionally low for anti-spam/DoS (not revenue), notes the small per-operation base fee (100 stroops / 0.00001 XLM), explains fees are burned rather than paid to validators or the Foundation, and ties low fees to micropayment/cross-border use cases with surge pricing as the safety valve.
failIf: It says transaction fees are paid to or profit validators / the SDF.
It claims the purpose of low fees is revenue or competition rather than spam/DoS prevention.
It states fees are a fixed flat amount that can never change, denying surge pricing / the per-operation bidding mechanism.
inconclusiveIf: It says fees are 'very low' and good for payments but never mentions the anti-spam/DoS purpose or that fees are burned (not validator revenue).
It gives the use-case implications but omits the actual fee mechanism (per-operation base fee and/or surge pricing).
canonicalFacts: Stellar fees are deliberately low because their purpose is anti-spam / denial-of-service prevention, not revenue generation.

The protocol charges a small base/minimum fee per operation: 100 stroops, where 1 stroop = 0.0000001 XLM, so the minimum is 0.00001 XLM per operation (the 2019 SDF post pegged 100 stroops at ~0.00008 US cents — figure is indicative/historical).
Fees are NOT paid to validators or the SDF — they are burned, so there is no profit incentive to raise them; validators set the minimum fee through SCP consensus.

Implications: near-zero predictable fees enable micropayments, cross-border/remittance, and machine/agent payments (e.g. x402); fees stay flexible via dynamic surge pricing (transactions bid fee-per-operation when a ledger, currently capped at ~1,000 operations, is full — cap is indicative/varies) and Protocol 13 fee-bump transactions.
The downside: the same low fees let arbitrage/spam bots cheaply clog ledgers, which is the main reason the SDF has weighed raising the minimum fee.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The rationale for low fees (anti-spam/DoS, not revenue), the burn-not-paid-to-validators mechanism, the per-operation base fee, and surge pricing are stable protocol facts; only specific USD equivalents and the exact ledger operation cap vary over time.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable as a stable protocol fact (standards skill); the SDF posts 'Should we increase the minimum fee?', 'Surge Pricing on Stellar: FAQ', and 'How We Simplified Fees on the Stellar Network' are the canonical sources.
Any USD-cent figure or ledger operation cap should be treated as indicative/time-varying, not a fixed quote.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Mention of fee-bump (Protocol 13) transactions letting a third party pay fees.
Note that validators set the minimum fee via SCP consensus.
Reference to the spam/arbitrage-bot downside the SDF has weighed when discussing raising the minimum.
niceToHave: Distinguishes the floor (base/minimum fee per operation) from the dynamic ceiling (surge-pricing bid when a ledger is full).
Frames fees as paid per operation rather than per transaction.

questions.question: What is a claimable balance on Stellar, how do I claim one (does claiming require a trustline or base reserve), and is it safe?

semantic.intent: Explain that a Stellar claimable balance is a protocol-level two-part payment: the sender locks funds into an intermediate ledger entry with claimant(s) and a claim predicate via CreateClaimableBalance, and a designated recipient later collects it with ClaimClaimableBalance. Clarify the trustline/base-reserve rules for claiming (XLM vs non-XLM) and that mechanically it is safe/non-custodial while unsolicited/unknown claimable balances warrant scam caution.
mustInclude: It is a two-part payment: the sender locks funds into an intermediate ledger entry via CreateClaimableBalance (tagged with one or more claimants and a claim predicate, e.g. time-based conditions) and a designated claimant later collects it with ClaimClaimableBalance.
An XLM claimable balance needs no trustline, but to claim a non-XLM asset the claimant must have (or first add) a trustline for that asset, which costs ~0.5 XLM base reserve unless sponsored.

The sender (not the recipient) pays the base reserve to create the entry; it is non-custodial, the entry cannot be silently deleted, and the sender's base reserve is returned when the balance is claimed.
Claiming itself is harmless/safe, but treat unknown or spam claimable balances with caution and do not trust or interact with the unknown asset or its issuer afterward.
mustAvoid: Claiming that receiving or claiming a claimable balance can itself drain your account or compromise your keys.
Stating that claiming a non-XLM asset requires no trustline, or that the recipient pays the base reserve to create the entry.
Treating a claimable balance as a custodial/centralized hold that a third party can revoke or delete at will.
passIf: The answer frames it as a two-part CreateClaimableBalance/ClaimClaimableBalance payment with claimant(s)+predicate, correctly states the XLM-vs-non-XLM trustline/base-reserve rule for claiming, and notes it is non-custodial while advising caution with unknown/spam balances.
failIf: It says claiming a non-XLM asset needs no trustline, or that the recipient must pay the base reserve to create the entry.
It claims claiming a (even unknown) claimable balance can drain funds or steal keys, rather than that the asset/issuer itself should not be trusted afterward.
It mischaracterizes the mechanism as custodial or as something a third party can delete/revoke.
inconclusiveIf: It defines a claimable balance only vaguely (e.g. 'a way to send funds') without the two-part operation/predicate structure.
It covers the mechanism but omits the trustline/base-reserve rule for claiming AND the safety guidance.
canonicalFacts: A claimable balance is a two-part payment (introduced in Protocol 15 / CAP-0023): CreateClaimableBalance locks funds into an intermediate ledger entry with one or more claimants and a claim predicate, and ClaimClaimableBalance lets a designated claimant collect it later.

Its purpose is to send a (typically non-XLM) asset to someone who lacks a trustline at send time; the sender pays the base reserve for the entry so the recipient is not blocked by trustline/reserve friction when the funds are sent.

To actually claim a non-XLM asset the claimant must still have or first add a trustline for that asset (a non-Lumen balance can only be recorded in a trustline); adding a trustline costs ~0.5 XLM base reserve (indicative; reserve-dependent) unless sponsored. XLM claimable balances need no trustline.

It is non-custodial: funds sit in a protocol-controlled entry that cannot be deleted, claimants can include the sender as a recoverable/refund path, and the sender's base reserve is returned when the entry is claimed.
Claiming an unknown/spam claimable balance is itself harmless, but standard Stellar scam-avoidance applies: do not interact with or trust the unknown asset/issuer afterward.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The claimable-balance mechanism, its operations, the trustline/base-reserve rules, and non-custodial/safety properties are stable protocol facts; only the exact base-reserve value could shift with network configuration.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from stable protocol facts / the standards skill (CAP-0023, Protocol 15); the ~0.5 XLM base reserve is reserve-config-dependent and should be framed as indicative.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Mention of claim predicates supporting time-based or compound conditions.
Note that the sender can be listed as a claimant to enable a refund/recovery path.
Reference that wallets surface incoming claimable balances and may let you ignore unknown ones.
niceToHave: Distinguishes the no-trustline-needed-at-send-time benefit from the still-need-a-trustline-to-claim-non-XLM rule.
Names sponsored reserves as the way to avoid the claimant paying the trustline base reserve.

questions.question: How do I create/issue my own custom asset (token) on Stellar, what does it cost, and why would I want to do it?

semantic.intent: Explain to a non-developer how a classic custom asset is issued on Stellar, what it actually costs, and why people do it. A correct answer walks the basic issuance flow, makes clear there is no protocol issuance fee (cost is just the trustline reserve plus tiny per-op fees), and conveys that the purpose is tokenizing value on a low-cost global ledger.
mustInclude: Describes the classic issuance flow: an issuing account whose public key permanently identifies the asset, a separate distribution account, a trustline from the receiver to the issuer for the new asset, and a payment of the new asset to distribute it (Stellar Laboratory or an SDK can be used).
States there is no protocol issuance fee; cost is negligible — each trustline locks a 0.5 XLM minimum-balance reserve (refundable if the trustline is removed) plus tiny per-operation network fees.

Explains the purpose is tokenization: putting value (stablecoins, RWAs/securities, community tokens, NFTs) on a low-cost, global, 24/7 ledger for liquidity, lower intermediary cost, and global access.
Notes that advanced/regulated assets can add compliance logic via Soroban token contracts (Stellar Asset Contract / SEP-41 token interface).
mustAvoid: Claiming Stellar charges a protocol fee or licensing cost just to issue an asset.
Saying you can issue and distribute an asset without any trustline or without a minimum-balance reserve.
Conflating a classic Stellar asset with deploying a custom Soroban smart contract as the only/default issuance path.
passIf: The answer conveys the four-step classic flow (issuer + distribution accounts, trustline, payment), states there is no issuance fee and the real cost is the ~0.5 XLM trustline reserve plus tiny fees, and frames the purpose as tokenization on a low-cost global ledger.
It at minimum makes the issuance mechanism, the negligible/reserve-based cost, and the tokenization purpose all clear, even if SEP-41/SAC is omitted.
failIf: It claims there is a protocol issuance fee or that issuing a token is expensive on Stellar.
It badly mischaracterizes the mechanism (e.g., no issuer/distribution accounts, no trustline) such that following it would not produce a working asset.
It says the reserve is permanently lost / non-refundable, or omits the trustline requirement entirely.
inconclusiveIf: It mentions issuing a token but gives neither the cost (no fee + ~0.5 XLM reserve) nor a usable description of the trustline/issuer-distribution mechanism.
It explains cost or purpose only and never describes how issuance actually works.
canonicalFacts: Issuing a classic Stellar asset is roughly four steps (via Stellar Laboratory or an SDK): create an issuing account whose public key identifies the asset, create a separate distribution account (issuer ideally in cold storage), have the receiving account add a trustline to the issuer for the asset, then pay/send the new asset from the issuer to distribute it.
There is no protocol issuance fee; cost is just the 0.5 XLM trustline minimum-balance reserve (indicative; refundable if the trustline is removed) plus fractions-of-a-cent per-operation network fees.

The purpose is tokenization — representing fiat-backed stablecoins, RWAs/securities, community tokens, or NFTs on a global, low-cost, 24/7 ledger for liquidity, lower intermediary cost, global access, and transparent ownership.
Advanced/regulated assets can add compliance logic via Soroban token contracts (Stellar Asset Contract / SEP-41 Token Interface).

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The classic issuance mechanism, the absence of an issuance fee, and the tokenization rationale are stable protocol facts; the 0.5 XLM base reserve is a long-standing network parameter, only marginally subject to change.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the assets skill / stable protocol knowledge; the issuance flow, reserve, and SAC/SEP-41 path are durable facts, not live data.
The 0.5 XLM per-trustline reserve is a network parameter and should be treated as indicative.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Security best practice of keeping the issuing account in cold storage and using the distribution account for day-to-day operations.
Mention of authorization flags / clawback / regulated-asset controls (SEP-8 / auth_required).
Concrete pointer to Stellar Laboratory or a specific SDK call for creating the asset.
niceToHave: Distinguishes the one-time-ish trustline reserve cost from the recurring per-operation fee.
Clarifies that the asset code plus issuer public key together uniquely identify the asset.

questions.question: I'm a regular user with USDC on Ethereum (ERC-20) or sitting on an exchange. How do I get it onto the Stellar network, and what fees should I expect?

semantic.intent: Help a non-developer end user get USDC from Ethereum/ERC-20 or a centralized exchange onto Stellar. Lay out the practical routes (CCTP bridge and exchange withdrawal) and set fee expectations honestly, distinguishing near-zero Stellar-side fees from separately-charged Ethereum gas and exchange withdrawal fees, and clarifying that USDC on Stellar is natively issued by Circle (not wrapped).
mustInclude: Covers the centralized-exchange path: deposit USDC to an exchange that lists USDC on Stellar (e.g. Kraken, Coinbase, Bitso) and withdraw by selecting the 'Stellar' network.
Covers cross-chain bridging via Circle's CCTP (native burn-and-mint, 1:1, no wrapped/custodial token), reachable through tools such as Allbridge Next or crossmesh.
Notes USDC on Stellar is natively issued by Circle and fungible with USDC on Ethereum, not a wrapped/bridged token.
Sets fee expectations: Stellar-side network fees are a fraction of a cent with ~seconds settlement, while Ethereum gas and per-exchange withdrawal fees are charged separately and vary.
mustAvoid: Claiming USDC on Stellar is a wrapped, bridged, or custodial synthetic token rather than Circle-native and fungible with Ethereum USDC.
Quoting a single fixed dollar figure for Ethereum gas or exchange withdrawal fees as if it were stable.
Inventing a route that does not exist (e.g. a generic lock-and-mint third-party bridge that mints wrapped USDC) as the recommended path.
passIf: The answer gives at least one concrete working route (CCTP bridge or exchange 'Stellar' network withdrawal), correctly frames USDC on Stellar as Circle-native/fungible, and sets honest fee expectations (near-zero Stellar-side vs separate Ethereum gas / exchange withdrawal fees).
failIf: It characterizes USDC on Stellar as a wrapped/bridged/custodial token.
It claims a fixed total fee or that Stellar-side fees include the Ethereum gas / exchange withdrawal cost.
It gives no actionable route to move the funds onto Stellar.
inconclusiveIf: It mentions moving USDC to Stellar in general terms but names no concrete route (neither CCTP bridge nor an exchange's Stellar withdrawal) and gives no fee framing.
It addresses only fees or only the native-issuance point without offering a usable path.
evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the stellar-ecosystem-digest corpus: Circle CCTP-on-Stellar launch and exchange USDC-on-Stellar listings; cite the corpus item date since exchange support and bridge tooling evolve.
Stellar near-zero base fee and ~seconds settlement are stable protocol facts; specific Ethereum gas and exchange withdrawal fees are indicative and time-varying, not fixed figures.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: Which exchanges list USDC on Stellar, available CCTP-bridge tooling, and the set of CCTP-connected chains change over time, so a current corpus source is needed; the near-zero Stellar fee and Circle-native fungibility are stable.

canonicalFacts: Circle's CCTP went live on Stellar in May 2026, connecting it to 23 chains including Ethereum, Base and Solana (chain count is indicative and may grow); it burns native USDC on the source chain and mints it 1:1 on the destination with no wrapped assets or custodial bridge, settling in seconds. Accessible through tools such as Allbridge Next or crossmesh.xyz.

Centralized exchanges that list USDC on Stellar (e.g. Kraken, Coinbase, Bitso) let you withdraw to Stellar by selecting the 'Stellar' network when withdrawing (the specific exchange list is indicative and changes).
USDC on Stellar is natively issued by Circle (not a wrapped or bridged token) and is fungible with USDC on Ethereum.

On the Stellar side, network fees are a fraction of a cent (near-zero) and settlement takes ~3-5 seconds; Ethereum/ERC-20 gas cost and any per-exchange withdrawal fee are charged separately and vary (no fixed figure).
allowedExtraNotRequired: Mentioning that a Stellar account/wallet needs to exist (and may require a minimum reserve) to receive the funds.
Noting which route is cheaper or faster depending on whether the user starts from on-chain ERC-20 USDC versus funds already on an exchange.
Listing additional CCTP-connected chains or additional supporting exchanges.
niceToHave: Distinguishes the bridge route (CCTP, on-chain, for users holding ERC-20 USDC) from the exchange route (for users whose USDC sits on a CEX).
Reminds the user to double-check the destination address and network selection before withdrawing.

questions.question: Where can I view Stellar network metrics and activity charts — TPS, fees, transaction volume, decentralization, DeFi liquidity, and dollar value?

semantic.intent: Point a non-developer user to the right Stellar metrics/explorer tools for each kind of stat they want — general network performance/throughput/fees/volume, node decentralization, and DeFi liquidity/dollar value — naming concrete tools rather than describing how to query the chain directly.
mustInclude: Recommends Stellar Expert (stellar.expert) for block-explorer and network stats — throughput/TPS, fees, asset price history, and payment/transaction volume.
Recommends Stellarbeat (stellarbeat.io) for decentralization — node and quorum/organization health and network topology.
Recommends a DeFi analytics dashboard such as the Stellar DeFi Dune dashboards (dune.com) for TVL, trading volume, liquidity, and dollar-value metrics.
Distinguishes which tool answers which metric category (network/protocol stats vs node decentralization vs DeFi TVL/liquidity) rather than naming one catch-all source.
mustAvoid: Claiming a single tool covers all of network stats, decentralization, and DeFi liquidity (e.g. saying Stellar Expert shows node decentralization or DeFi TVL).
Telling a non-developer they must run RPC/Horizon queries or write code to get these metrics instead of pointing to ready dashboards.
Inventing tool names or URLs that don't exist for these metric categories.
passIf: Names Stellar Expert for general network/throughput/fees/volume AND Stellarbeat for decentralization AND a DeFi analytics dashboard (Dune) for TVL/liquidity, with the categories correctly matched.
failIf: Recommends only one catch-all source for all metric categories, or misattributes a category (e.g. says Stellar Expert shows node decentralization or DeFi TVL).
Answers only with how to query the chain via RPC/Horizon/SDK rather than naming concrete dashboards/explorers.
Names no real, correct tool for any of the three categories.
inconclusiveIf: Names one or two correct tools (e.g. only Stellar Expert) but omits a whole metric pillar such as decentralization or DeFi liquidity.
Mentions explorers/dashboards generically without naming the concrete tools mapped to the metric categories.
canonicalFacts: Stellar Expert (stellar.expert) is a comprehensive block explorer and analytics platform with detailed statistics, price history, analytic reports, and network performance metrics (throughput/TPS, fees, payment/transaction volume).
Stellarbeat (stellarbeat.io) is a network explorer that lists all nodes and organizations, shows quorum/decentralization health, and lets you simulate network topologies.
The Stellar DeFi Dune Dashboards (dune.com, e.g. dune.com/paltalabs) provide independent, verifiable DeFi metrics including TVL, trading volume, liquidity, and protocol comparisons.
Stellarchain, Steexp/Steepx, and Token Terminal are additional explorers/analytics platforms covering Stellar (indicative — the set of available tools evolves).

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The specific tools that catalog Stellar metrics can change (new dashboards appear, ones get deprecated), so the live directory grounds which named tools to recommend; the metric categories themselves are stable.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the LumenLoop ecosystem directory (Infrastructure & Services, Analytics/Block Explorer tags) via the data / ecosystem-scout skill; the named tools and their stated purposes come from the directory listing.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Mentioning additional explorers/analytics platforms in the directory (Stellarchain, Steexp/Steepx, Token Terminal).
Noting that specific live figures (TPS, TVL, dollar values) are time-varying and read off the dashboards rather than stated as fixed numbers.
niceToHave: A one-line mapping of metric-to-tool so the user knows exactly where to click for each stat.
A direct link such as dune.com/paltalabs for the DeFi dashboards.

questions.question: Which Stellar wallets support holding, swapping into, and sending USDC (and other stablecoins)?

semantic.intent: Help a retail user pick a Stellar wallet that can hold, send, and swap USDC (and other stablecoins). A correct answer names current consumer wallets, explains that USDC/EURC are native Stellar assets that need a trustline to the issuer, and points to how swapping is done (in-wallet swap or via the Stellar DEX / aggregators).
mustInclude: USDC (and EURC) are native Stellar assets, so any standard Stellar wallet can hold/send/receive them once a trustline to the issuer is added (each trustline costs a ~0.5 XLM reserve, which some wallets sponsor).
Names current consumer wallets that work, e.g. Freighter, xBull, Lobstr, Hana, HOT Wallet, or Vibrant.

Swapping is done either via wallets with built-in swaps (e.g. xBull Swap, HOT Wallet) or by routing through the Stellar DEX / aggregators such as Soroswap, StellarBroker, or Allbridge (cross-chain USDC via Circle CCTP).
mustAvoid: Claiming USDC on Stellar requires a bridge or wrapped token to hold/send it natively, or that it is not a native Stellar asset.
Saying you can hold USDC without any trustline, or that the trustline reserve is free for all wallets (it is ~0.5 XLM unless a wallet sponsors it).
Recommending a wallet that does not actually support Stellar (e.g. naming an EVM-only wallet).
passIf: The answer names at least one or two real current Stellar consumer wallets (e.g. Freighter / xBull / Lobstr / Hana / HOT Wallet / Vibrant), states USDC/EURC are native assets needing a trustline, and points to a concrete way to swap (in-wallet swap or Stellar DEX / aggregator like Soroswap).
failIf: It says USDC on Stellar must be bridged/wrapped to be held or sent natively.
It omits the trustline requirement entirely or claims no trustline is needed to hold USDC.
It recommends wallets that don't support Stellar or invents non-existent wallets.
inconclusiveIf: It names a wallet or two but never mentions the trustline requirement nor any swap path.
It speaks generically about 'Stellar wallets support stablecoins' without naming a specific current wallet or a concrete swap route.
evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the LumenLoop directory/corpus (stellar-ecosystem-scout); specific wallet feature sets and aggregator lists are corpus-grounded (articles 1843 and 831) and may evolve over time.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Non-custodial / hardware options like Stellarport, Unstoppable Wallet, OneKey, or Interstellar.
Notes on HOT Wallet's one-tap USDC/EURC receive/send, built-in swaps, and free trustline activation.
Naming additional aggregators/DEXs such as StellarX or StellarBroker.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: Wallet availability, feature sets (built-in swaps, trustline sponsorship), and the aggregator/bridge landscape change over time, so the specific named wallets and swap routes should be grounded in current corpus data rather than recalled from memory.

canonicalFacts: USDC and EURC are native Stellar assets; any standard Stellar wallet can hold, send, and receive them once a trustline to the issuer is added.
Each trustline costs a ~0.5 XLM reserve (indicative; some wallets sponsor it so the user pays nothing).
Commonly cited consumer wallets in the corpus: Freighter, xBull (with built-in xBull Swap), Lobstr, Hana, HOT Wallet, and Vibrant.

Swapping USDC is done via wallets with embedded swaps (xBull Swap, HOT Wallet) or through Stellar DEX / aggregators such as Soroswap, StellarBroker, StellarX, and Allbridge/Estrela (cross-chain USDC now via Circle CCTP).
HOT Wallet is documented as supporting one-tap USDC/EURC/XLM receive, send, built-in swaps, and free trustline activation.
niceToHave: Distinguishes wallets with built-in swaps from those that require routing through the DEX / an aggregator.
Notes that cross-chain USDC into Stellar now goes via Circle CCTP.

questions.question: How do I recover or restore my XLM in another wallet using a recovery phrase or secret key, and which wallets support importing them?

semantic.intent: Explain that a Stellar account is controlled by a keypair and that most non-custodial wallets let the user restore/import that same account in a new wallet from either the recovery (mnemonic) phrase or the raw secret key, naming specific wallets that support this and flagging the key format caveats.
mustInclude: A Stellar account is controlled by a keypair, so importing the recovery phrase or secret key into another non-custodial wallet restores access to the same account.
You can restore from either the multi-word recovery/mnemonic phrase or the raw secret key (the string beginning with 'S').
Names self-custody Stellar wallets that support importing an existing account, such as Freighter, LOBSTR, xBull, Albedo, or hardware wallets like Ledger.
Notes the caveat that a recovery phrase can derive many accounts while a single secret key restores only one, and not every wallet accepts both formats.
mustAvoid: Claiming you need the original wallet app or that the account can only be recovered in the wallet that created it.
Telling the user to share/enter their secret key or recovery phrase into a website, support agent, or any third party.
Asserting custodial-exchange-style 'account recovery' (email/password/KYC) restores a self-custody Stellar account.
passIf: The answer ties recovery to the keypair, says either the recovery phrase or the secret key can restore the same account into another non-custodial wallet, and names at least one concrete importing wallet (e.g. Freighter/LOBSTR/xBull).
failIf: It says a Stellar account can only be restored in the original wallet, or that no other wallet can import the phrase/secret key.
It conflates this with custodial/exchange password recovery, or advises disclosing the phrase/secret key to a third party.
It claims a single secret key recovers all of a phrase's derived accounts (or otherwise mischaracterizes the phrase-vs-secret-key distinction).
inconclusiveIf: It mentions a recovery phrase or secret key generally but never states that the same account can be imported into a different non-custodial wallet, or names no specific wallet.
It describes only one of the two formats and omits that both phrase and raw secret key are valid restore inputs without flagging the difference.
canonicalFacts: A Stellar account is controlled by a keypair; whoever holds the secret key (or a phrase that derives it) controls the account, independent of which wallet app is used.
Most non-custodial Stellar wallets let you restore/import an existing account from either the multi-word recovery (mnemonic) phrase or the raw secret key (the string beginning with 'S').
Self-custody wallets that support importing an existing account include Freighter, LOBSTR, xBull, Albedo, StellarTerm/Solar, and hardware wallets like Ledger/OneKey (set varies over time).
A recovery phrase can derive many accounts, whereas a single secret key restores only one account, and not every wallet accepts both formats (e.g. some wallets only support phrase-derived import).
SEP-30 recovery wallets (e.g. Vesseo) intentionally use a different recovery mechanism such as phone-number recovery instead of a phrase.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The keypair model and phrase/secret-key restore mechanics are stable protocol-level facts; only the exact roster of wallets supporting a given import format drifts slowly and is not point-in-time critical.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from stable Stellar protocol facts (keypair-controlled accounts) plus the directory/scout skill for the current set of importing wallets; LOBSTR support docs and the stellar.org 'Wallets to Store and Send XLM' guide are corroborating references.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Security warning to never share or type the phrase/secret key into untrusted sites or support staff.
Mention that hardware-wallet accounts are restored via the device's own seed rather than re-typing the secret key.
Note that some wallets currently support only phrase-derived import and list standalone secret-key import as a future feature.
niceToHave: Distinguishes a recovery phrase (derives multiple accounts) from a single secret key (one account) explicitly.
Points to the canonical wallets directory so the user can browse current options.

questions.question: Does a Ledger or other hardware wallet actually hold my Stellar private keys, and at what investment size is one worth it?

semantic.intent: Confirm that a hardware wallet (Ledger, Trezor, etc.) is non-custodial cold storage that generates and keeps the Stellar private keys offline on the device's secure element so the vendor never holds them, and give the practical hot-vs-cold tiering framing for when one is worth using rather than inventing a specific dollar threshold.
mustInclude: A hardware wallet (Ledger, Trezor, etc.) is non-custodial: the private keys are generated and stored offline on the device (secure element) and never leave it, transactions are signed on-device, and the vendor holds no copy of your keys or seed phrase.
Stellar is supported by hardware wallets, e.g. Ledger Live's native XLM/USDC support and use with Stellar clients (e.g. StellarTerm).
There is no specific dollar or holding-size threshold at which a hardware wallet becomes worth it; the right choice depends on the individual use case.
Practical guidance is to separate storage tiers: use a hot/software wallet (Freighter, LOBSTR, xBull) for small/active funds and move long-term savings to cold/hardware storage.
mustAvoid: Claiming the hardware wallet vendor (Ledger, etc.) holds, stores, or can access a copy of your private keys or seed phrase, or treating it as custodial.
Inventing a specific dollar amount or holding-size threshold at which a hardware wallet becomes 'worth it' as if it were an established fact.
Saying Stellar/XLM is not supported by hardware wallets.
passIf: The answer states the device is non-custodial with keys generated/stored offline on-device (vendor holds no copy), confirms Stellar/XLM support, and gives the hot-vs-cold tiering framing without asserting a fixed dollar threshold.
failIf: It says the hardware wallet maker holds or can recover your keys/seed, or otherwise treats the device as custodial.
It asserts a specific dollar threshold (e.g. 'worth it above $X') as a known fact.
It claims Stellar/XLM is unsupported by hardware wallets.
inconclusiveIf: It confirms keys stay on-device but omits the threshold/use-case point, or gives the threshold framing but never establishes that keys never leave the device (non-custodial).
canonicalFacts: A Ledger (and other hardware wallets like Trezor, OneKey, Hito, AirGap) is non-custodial: Stellar private keys are generated and stored offline on the device's secure element and never leave it; transactions are signed on-device and the vendor holds no copy and cannot access your seed phrase.
Ledger Live integrated native XLM/USDC support, and hardware wallets work with Stellar clients such as StellarTerm.
No source in the corpus specifies a dollar or holding-size threshold at which a hardware wallet becomes 'worth it'; SDF's guidance is only that the safest wallet depends on each individual use case.
Recommended practice is separating long-term/cold storage from a hot/software wallet (Freighter, LOBSTR, xBull) used for trading and daily transactions.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: Hardware-wallet custody semantics, Stellar support, and the hot-vs-cold tiering guidance are stable facts; no live point-in-time data is needed to answer.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the LumenLoop corpus / stable wallet-security facts; the non-custodial and tiering points are durable, and the absence of a dollar threshold is itself the corpus-grounded finding.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Naming additional supported hardware wallets (Trezor, OneKey, Hito, AirGap).
Mentioning the seed-phrase backup/recovery responsibility that comes with self-custody.
Noting passkey/smart-account alternatives as a separate self-custody path.
niceToHave: Frames the decision by threat model / amount-at-risk and convenience rather than a fixed number.
Distinguishes secure-element on-device signing from merely 'offline' to explain why keys are protected.

questions.question: What are good news sources and resources to stay up to date on Stellar (XLM) news, updates, and learning?

semantic.intent: Point a retail/newcomer user to a useful mix of places to follow Stellar (XLM) news and to learn, rather than a single canonical feed. A correct answer should name official SDF channels, at least one community hub, and at least one hands-on learning resource.
mustInclude: Names official Stellar Development Foundation sources (e.g. @StellarOrg on X, the stellar.org blog, or the developer docs/tools at developers.stellar.org / Stellar Laboratory).
Names at least one community hub (a Stellar Discord, the GalacticTalk forum, or Stellar Global).
Names at least one hands-on learning resource (e.g. Stellar Quest, stellarpro.dev, or Dapp World).
Frames the answer as a mix of source types, not one single canonical feed (an ecosystem aggregator like Lumen Loop or an explorer like Stellar Expert is a fine optional add).
mustAvoid: Presenting one single source as the only/canonical place for all Stellar news and learning.
Pointing the user primarily to price/trading or paid-shill sources instead of official + community + learning channels.
Inventing non-existent official channels or fabricating URLs.
passIf: The answer names at least one official SDF source, at least one community hub, and at least one learning resource, and frames them as a complementary mix.
It covers the three pillars (official, community, learning) with real Stellar-specific destinations even if the exact named examples differ from the reference.
failIf: It names only a single source (e.g. just stellar.org) and presents it as sufficient.
It omits any official SDF channel entirely, or substitutes generic crypto/price sites for Stellar-specific resources.
It fabricates a channel, handle, or URL that does not exist.
inconclusiveIf: It lists some Stellar resources but covers only one of the three pillars (e.g. official channels only, with no community hub or learning resource).
It stays vague ('follow the official channels and the community') without naming concrete destinations.
canonicalFacts: Official SDF channels are the canonical starting point: @StellarOrg on X (which posts weekly round-up threads), the stellar.org blog, and the developer docs/tools at developers.stellar.org plus Stellar Laboratory.
Community hubs include the official Stellar Dev Discord and a large community Discord, Stellar Global (stellarglobal.community / @stellarglobal_), and the GalacticTalk forum (galactictalk.org).
Hands-on learning resources include Stellar Quest (gamified Stellar Classic + Soroban challenges), stellarpro.dev, and Dapp World.

For ecosystem-wide aggregation Lumen Loop (lumenloop.com) pulls Stellar news, projects, events and a weekly roundup into one place; the Stellar Update blog (stellarupdate.com) covers ecosystem news; Stellar Expert (stellar.expert) is the go-to block explorer/analytics.
No single source is mandatory — a good answer names a mix of (a) official SDF sources, (b) at least one community Discord/forum, and (c) a learning resource.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The set of canonical Stellar news/community/learning destinations is stable; specific recent headlines change but the recommended sources do not, so no live lookup is required.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the stellar-ecosystem-digest skill / stable ecosystem knowledge of where Stellar publishes news, community, and learning content.
Named destinations should be real Stellar channels (handles/URLs from the canonical set), not invented ones.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Mentioning an ecosystem aggregator (Lumen Loop) or the Stellar Update blog.
Mentioning Stellar Expert as the explorer/analytics source.
Brief notes on what each source is best for (news vs. community vs. building).
niceToHave: Organizes the recommendations by pillar (official / community / learning) for clarity.
Calls out the @StellarOrg weekly round-up as an easy single-follow for ongoing updates.

questions.question: How do you explain Stellar Lumens (XLM) simply to a non-technical person?

semantic.intent: Give a plain-language, jargon-free explanation of what XLM/lumens are: the native digital token of the Stellar payment network, the practical jobs it does (paying tiny per-transaction fees, serving as a small minimum account balance/reserve, and acting as a bridge currency between otherwise-illiquid assets), plus basic supply facts — accessible to a non-technical reader.
mustInclude: XLM (lumens) is the native token/currency of the Stellar network, an open, fast, low-cost payment network for moving money across borders and expanding financial access.
XLM pays the network's small per-transaction fees and is held as a minimum account balance (base reserve), which together deter spam and keep the network efficient.
XLM can act as a bridge/intermediary currency so two otherwise-illiquid assets or currencies can be exchanged.
Lumens were created at launch (no mining); annual inflation was ended by community vote (2019) and a 2019 burn left a now-fixed supply of roughly 50 billion.
mustAvoid: Describing XLM as mined or earned via proof-of-work/proof-of-stake.
Conflating Stellar/XLM with a smart-contract gas token whose primary purpose is computation, or implying XLM is a stablecoin pegged to a fiat currency.
Drowning a non-technical reader in jargon (consensus internals, Soroban, SCP) instead of plain explanation.
passIf: The answer plainly identifies XLM as Stellar's native token for a cross-border payment network, names at least the fee + minimum-balance role (and ideally the bridge-currency role), and stays accessible to a non-technical reader.
It correctly frames XLM as created at launch (not mined) with a fixed ~50B supply, even if exact dates are omitted.
failIf: It says XLM is mined or that you earn it through staking/mining rewards.
It mischaracterizes XLM's core purpose (e.g., calls it a stablecoin, a pure gas-for-computation token, or a company equity).
It gives a wrong supply story (e.g., claims supply is still 100B, or that inflation is ongoing).
inconclusiveIf: It vaguely calls XLM 'Stellar's cryptocurrency' but never explains the fee/minimum-balance or bridge roles.
It explains the token but omits any supply/creation context (mining vs created-at-launch, ~50B).
canonicalFacts: XLM (lumens) is the native digital token of the Stellar network, a fast, low-cost open payment network built to move money across borders and expand financial access.

XLM pays the network's tiny transaction fees (0.00001 XLM each, indicative) and is held as a small minimum account balance (1 XLM base reserve, indicative) — together deterring spam and keeping the network efficient.
XLM can serve as a 'bridge' currency so two otherwise-illiquid assets or currencies can be exchanged.

Lumens were created entirely at launch (no mining); 100 billion existed initially, annual inflation was ended by community vote (October 2019), and a November 2019 burn cut the total to a now-fixed ~50 billion.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: What XLM is, the jobs it does, and the post-2019 fixed ~50B supply are stable protocol/history facts; no live data is needed for a non-technical explainer.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable as a stable protocol/history fact (Stellar Development Foundation 'Stellar Lumens', stellar.org/learn/lumens; LumenLoop article id 49 / slug stellar-lumens).
Exact fee/reserve figures are indicative defaults and the ~50B supply is a fixed historical outcome — none require a live lookup.
allowedExtraNotRequired: An everyday analogy (e.g., XLM as digital cash / 'fuel' for the network) to aid a non-technical reader.
Mention of the path-payment / DEX mechanism behind bridge-currency conversion.
Note that XLM has a market price and trades on exchanges.
niceToHave: Frames the explanation around a relatable use case like cheap, fast remittances.
Notes the original 100B → ~50B reduction explicitly to ground the supply fact.

questions.question: Can I stake my XLM, and are there plans for native staking on Stellar?

semantic.intent: Tell a retail holder that XLM has no protocol-level staking and there are no announced plans for native staking, explain the consensus-design reason (SCP / proof-of-agreement, not proof-of-stake), and clarify that anything marketed as 'staking XLM' is off-protocol.
mustInclude: XLM cannot be staked at the protocol level; there is no native staking and no announced plans for it.

Stellar reaches consensus via the Stellar Consensus Protocol (SCP / 'proof-of-agreement'), not proof-of-stake, so there is no token staking, slashing, or protocol yield tied to block production; validators earn a seat through trust/reputation, not stake.
The one historical native yield mechanism — the ~1% annual 'inflation' pool — was disabled by SDF in 2019.
What people loosely call 'staking XLM' is always off-protocol: centralized-exchange custodial reward products or ecosystem DeFi yield (lending, LP rewards, vaults), none of which are native staking.
mustAvoid: Claiming Stellar has native XLM staking / proof-of-stake rewards, or that you can stake XLM for protocol yield.
Presenting exchange 'staking' or DeFi yield products as if they were native protocol staking, or implying native staking is planned/coming.
Asserting the inflation pool still pays out (it was disabled in 2019).
passIf: The answer says no native staking and no plans, attributes consensus to SCP/proof-of-agreement (not PoS), and frames any 'staking XLM' product as off-protocol (exchange custodial or DeFi yield).
failIf: It says you can stake XLM for native/protocol rewards or that Stellar is proof-of-stake.
It claims native staking is planned/on the roadmap, or that the inflation pool still pays a native yield.
inconclusiveIf: It says you can't stake XLM but never explains why (no SCP/PoS distinction) nor distinguishes off-protocol products from native staking.
It only mentions exchange/DeFi 'staking' options without stating there is no native protocol staking.
evidenceRequirements: Stable protocol fact: Stellar's SCP consensus and the absence of native staking are durable; answerable from the standards skill / protocol knowledge, not live data.
The inflation-pool disablement (2019) and existence of off-protocol yield categories are historical/structural facts, not point-in-time figures.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: Stellar's consensus design (SCP, no PoS), the absence of native staking, and the 2019 inflation disablement are stable protocol facts; only the specific off-protocol yield products and their rates vary over time.

canonicalFacts: XLM cannot be staked at the protocol level and there is no native staking, with no announced plans for it.

Stellar uses the Stellar Consensus Protocol (SCP / 'proof-of-agreement'), not proof-of-stake: validators earn a seat via trust and reputation, with no token staking, no slashing, and no protocol yield tied to block production.
The historical ~1% annual native 'inflation' yield pool was disabled by SDF in 2019.

Anything marketed as 'staking XLM' is off-protocol: centralized-exchange custodial reward products or ecosystem DeFi yield (lending, LP rewards, vaults such as Upshift's earnXLM) — examples are indicative and change over time.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Naming concrete historical examples of exchange custodial products (e.g. Binance/Crypto.com circa 2019–2020) or current DeFi yield avenues.
Risk note that off-protocol custodial/DeFi yield carries counterparty or smart-contract risk unlike native protocol staking.
niceToHave: Brief contrast of how a seat in SCP (trust-based) differs from stake-weighted PoS validation.
Pointer that DeFi yield on XLM is earned by supplying assets, not by securing consensus.

questions.question: What is the point of XLM (lumens)? Is it like Ethereum gas, and how does the native asset differ from other Stellar tokens like USDC?

semantic.intent: Explain what XLM (the lumen) is actually for — paying the tiny flat per-transaction base fee and meeting account minimum-balance reserves, primarily as anti-spam friction — and contrast it carefully with both Ethereum gas (similar in that it pays fees, but flat/negligible rather than metered by computation) and issued tokens like USDC (which require an issuer and a trustline, whereas XLM requires neither).
mustInclude: XLM is Stellar's native asset and the only token requiring no issuer and no trustline; issued assets like USDC must be issued by an account and require the holder to establish a trustline.

XLM pays the tiny flat per-transaction base fee (~0.00001 XLM / 100 stroops) and satisfies the account minimum-balance / base reserve, which grows per additional ledger entry such as a trustline or offer.
Its core design purpose is anti-spam friction — a modest barrier that deters flooding the ledger and abusing network resources — not a metered compute/gas unit priced by arbitrary computation.

It is only loosely analogous to Ethereum gas: like gas it pays fees, but Stellar fees are flat and negligible rather than priced per opcode; XLM also serves as a bridge/medium of exchange and the network's reserve asset.
mustAvoid: Claiming XLM is metered/priced per computation like EVM gas, or that Stellar fees scale with contract complexity the way Ethereum gas does.
Saying issued tokens like USDC also need no issuer/trustline, or that XLM requires a trustline to hold.
Framing XLM purely as a speculative/national currency while omitting its fee + reserve + anti-spam role.
passIf: The answer identifies XLM as the native, issuer-less/trustline-less asset, names its fee + minimum-balance/reserve role and anti-spam purpose, and draws the loose (not exact) gas analogy with the flat-vs-metered distinction.
It correctly contrasts XLM with USDC on the issuer/trustline requirement while covering the fee/reserve/anti-spam function.
failIf: It states XLM is a metered gas unit priced by computation like Ethereum, with no caveat that Stellar fees are flat/negligible.
It claims USDC needs no trustline/issuer or that XLM does require one — i.e., it inverts the native-vs-issued distinction.
It omits the fee/reserve/anti-spam purpose entirely and treats XLM only as a tradable currency.
inconclusiveIf: It mentions XLM pays fees but never explains the minimum-balance/reserve or anti-spam purpose, nor the issuer/trustline contrast with issued tokens.
It compares XLM to gas without clarifying whether the analogy is exact or loose (flat vs metered).
canonicalFacts: XLM is Stellar's native asset and the only token that needs no issuer and no trustline; issued assets (USDC, custom/RWA tokens) are issued by an account and require a trustline to hold.

Every account must hold a small XLM minimum balance — a base reserve (historically 1 XLM) plus an incremental reserve (0.5 XLM, indicative/governable) per additional ledger entry such as a trustline or offer.
The base transaction fee is ~0.00001 XLM (100 stroops, indicative); fees are flat and negligible, not priced by arbitrary computation/EVM opcodes.

XLM's core design purpose is anti-spam friction — a modest barrier deterring ledger flooding and resource abuse — and it additionally serves as a built-in bridge/medium of exchange and the network's reserve asset.
Supply: 100B created at genesis; 1% annual inflation ended by community vote in Oct 2019 and supply was reduced to ~50B in Nov 2019, with no further issuance (figures indicative/historical).

freshnessPolicy.rationale: XLM's role (fees, reserves, anti-spam), the native-vs-issued/trustline distinction, and the gas contrast are stable protocol facts; only exact reserve/fee parameters can change via network config and supply figures are fixed history.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the assets skill and stable Stellar protocol knowledge (native asset, base fee, base/incremental reserve, anti-spam rationale); no live lookup needed.
Specific fee/reserve numbers are indicative network parameters and may be quoted as approximate.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Stroop definition (1 XLM = 10,000,000 stroops) and that fees/reserves are network-config parameters that can change by validator vote.
Supply history (100B genesis, inflation ended Oct 2019, ~50B after Nov 2019 burn).
Note that XLM acts as a path-payment bridge between otherwise illiquid issued assets.
niceToHave: Explicitly frames the gas comparison as 'similar in spirit, different in mechanism' rather than equating the two.
Mentions that reserves are refundable/locked (not spent) while fees are consumed.

questions.question: How decentralized is Stellar, and how does the network maintain and grow decentralization given SDF and its trusted validators?

semantic.intent: Explain Stellar's decentralization model and how it is being grown. A correct answer must describe SCP as a reputation-based Federated Byzantine Agreement system (each validator publicly chooses whom it trusts, not anonymous economic stake), explain that the network is anchored by a small set of publicly-identified tier-1 organizations, and convey SDF's role plus its ongoing effort to reduce its own centrality and onboard more validators.
mustInclude: Consensus is the Stellar Consensus Protocol, a Federated Byzantine Agreement / proof-of-agreement model where each validator publicly configures which other validators (its quorum set) it trusts, rather than relying on anonymous economic stake, PoW, or PoS.

Decentralization is anchored by a small number of real-world, publicly-identified tier-1 organizations whose reputation is at stake; the network can tolerate the failure of some of them before halting.
SDF was historically a central point of quorum intersection and has been working for years to reduce its own centrality by coordinating the onboarding of additional independent validators.
The roadmap grows decentralization by adding more tier-1 organizations (raising fault tolerance) and reducing correlated/geo-political failure, with validator trust configurations publicly auditable.
mustAvoid: Claiming Stellar uses proof-of-stake or proof-of-work, or that validators are selected/secured by anonymous economic stake or mining.
Asserting Stellar is fully decentralized / trustless with no concentration, OR conversely that it is centrally controlled by SDF with no path to broader decentralization.
Stating that consensus rewards or token staking secure the network.
passIf: The answer frames consensus as SCP/FBA with publicly-chosen quorum trust among identified tier-1 orgs, acknowledges SDF's historical centrality and its effort to reduce it, and conveys that decentralization is being grown by adding more orgs / reducing correlated failure.
failIf: It describes Stellar's consensus as PoS/PoW or stake/mining-based.
