It claims the network is secured by anonymous validators or that anyone can join consensus permissionlessly with no reputation/quorum-trust component.
It either denies any centralization concern entirely or claims SDF unilaterally controls consensus with no decentralization roadmap.
inconclusiveIf: It names SCP/FBA but never addresses SDF's centrality or how decentralization is being grown.
It discusses decentralization only in vague terms without identifying the reputation-based tier-1 / quorum-trust mechanism.
canonicalFacts: Stellar's consensus is the Stellar Consensus Protocol (SCP), a Federated Byzantine Agreement / 'proof-of-agreement' model: each validator publicly configures a quorum set of other validators it trusts, rather than securing the chain via anonymous economic stake or mining.

Validators are run by real-world, publicly-identified 'tier-1 organizations' whose reputation is on the line; the network is anchored by a small set of them (indicative: ~7 since Jan 2020) and can sustain the failure of some (indicative: ~2) before halting.
SDF was historically the single point of quorum intersection and has spent years coordinating validator onboarding to reduce its own centrality.

SDF's roadmap grows decentralization by adding more tier-1 orgs (indicative goal: '2x Tier 1 Resilience' to ~13 orgs, tolerating ~4 failures), enabled by overlay/consensus upgrades, stress-testing, and a transparent process for evaluating and adding orgs to quorum sets; exact counts and targets evolve over time.

SDF frames real decentralization as reducing correlated failure across geo-political, security, and mission-alignment dimensions rather than maximizing raw node count; validator trust configs are publicly auditable (e.g. radar.withobsrvr.com, stellar.expert).

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The mechanism (SCP/FBA, quorum trust, tier-1 model) is stable, but the specific tier-1 org count, current fault tolerance, and the roadmap target are point-in-time figures that evolve, so a live/dated source should ground any specific numbers.

evidenceRequirements: The mechanism is a stable protocol fact answerable from the standards skill; specific tier-1 counts, fault-tolerance numbers, and roadmap targets should be grounded in a dated source (e.g. SDF's decentralization explainers) since they vary.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Naming specific tier-1 organizations or the tools to audit quorum configs (radar.withobsrvr.com, stellar.expert).
Citing the relevant SDF explainer posts or Stellar Core overlay/consensus version upgrades.
Noting the distinction between node-count and correlated-failure framings of decentralization.
niceToHave: Gives concrete current figures (tier-1 count, failures tolerated, roadmap target) clearly marked as point-in-time.
Explains why quorum intersection / reputation-at-stake substitutes for economic stake in deterring misbehavior.

questions.question: How does Stellar's SCP consensus stay secure without mining or staking, and why don't 51% attacks or forks apply?

semantic.intent: Explain that Stellar's security comes from the Stellar Consensus Protocol (SCP), an instance of Federated Byzantine Agreement built on a web of trust of overlapping quorum slices of known, vouched-for validators rather than mining or staking, so there is no hashpower or economic-stake majority to seize and a classic 51% attack does not apply. Make clear SCP prioritizes safety over liveness: when no quorum can be reached the network halts rather than forking or confirming conflicting transactions.
mustInclude: Security is based on SCP / Federated Byzantine Agreement (a web of trust of overlapping quorum slices of known, vouched-for validators), not Proof-of-Work mining or Proof-of-Stake.
Because there is no hashpower or stake majority to acquire, a classic 51% attack does not apply.
SCP favors safety over liveness: if a quorum cannot be reached the network halts/pauses rather than forking or confirming conflicting transactions, which is what prevents forks and double-spends.

Robustness/DoS resistance comes from decentralization across many independently-run validators (including a set of reliable Tier 1 organizations), so the network keeps agreeing even if some nodes fail or misbehave.
mustAvoid: Claiming Stellar uses mining (PoW) or staking (PoS) for consensus, or that you stake XLM to validate.
Saying a 51%/majority-hashpower or majority-stake attack works the same way on Stellar as on Bitcoin/Ethereum.
Asserting that SCP keeps producing blocks (favors liveness) and so cannot halt, or that disagreement causes a chain fork/double-spend.
passIf: The answer attributes security to SCP/FBA with trust-based overlapping quorum slices (not mining/staking), explains why 51% attacks don't apply, and notes SCP halts rather than forking (safety over liveness).
failIf: It says Stellar secures itself via mining or staking, or that XLM is staked to validate.
It claims a classic 51% (hashpower or stake majority) attack applies to Stellar in the usual way.
It says SCP forks / double-spends instead of halting when consensus can't be reached, or claims it favors liveness over safety.
inconclusiveIf: It names SCP/FBA but never explains why 51% attacks don't apply or that the network halts instead of forking.
It says 'no mining or staking' without identifying SCP/quorum-slice trust or the safety-over-liveness halt behavior.
evidenceRequirements: Answerable as a stable protocol fact via the standards skill / SCP documentation; no live data needed.
Validator counts (e.g. ~500+ total nodes, ~23 Tier 1 validators) are indicative and slowly change over time, not load-bearing for the answer.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Mention of quorum intersection as the property that ties overlapping slices into network-wide agreement.
Note that validators are known/identifiable and must be vouched for rather than anonymous (proof-of-agreement).
Indicative validator counts (~500+ nodes, ~23 Tier 1 validators run by diverse organizations).
niceToHave: Frames the trade-off explicitly: SCP is provably safe and chooses to halt (lose liveness) over ever confirming conflicting transactions.
Contrasts with Nakamoto/PoW and PoS consensus to make the no-economic-majority point concrete.
canonicalFacts: Stellar's consensus is the Stellar Consensus Protocol (SCP), an implementation of Federated Byzantine Agreement (FBA) — a web of trust, not Proof-of-Work mining or Proof-of-Stake.

Each validator picks a quorum slice of nodes it trusts; network-wide consensus emerges from overlapping quorum slices joined by quorum intersection, and validators are known/vouched-for rather than anonymous.
There is no hashpower or stake economic majority to seize, so a classic 51% attack does not apply to Stellar.

SCP is provably safe and prioritizes safety over liveness: if a quorum cannot be reached the network halts/pauses rather than forking or confirming conflicting transactions, preventing forks and double-spends.

Robustness comes from decentralization across many independently-run validators (indicative: ~500+ total nodes including ~23 highly-reliable Tier 1 validators run by diverse organizations); counts vary over time.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: How SCP/FBA secures the network and why 51% attacks don't apply are stable protocol facts; only validator counts drift slowly and are indicative, not load-bearing.

questions.question: How is XLM supply structured — is there a max supply, was inflation removed, are fees burned, and does circulating supply shrink over time?

semantic.intent: Explain XLM's supply structure as an evergreen tokenomics concept: total supply is effectively fixed near 50 billion lumens after the SDF's 2019 burn, the original protocol inflation mechanism was disabled, Stellar does NOT burn transaction fees, and therefore circulating supply does not structurally shrink over time. A correct answer must convey the no-ongoing-burn / non-deflationary nature, not just quote numbers.
mustInclude: SDF burned roughly 55 billion XLM in 2019, cutting total supply from ~105 billion to ~50 billion, so total supply is now effectively fixed/capped near 50B with no further burns pledged.
Stellar's original ~1%/year inflation mechanism was disabled (Stellar Core protocol v12, late 2019) so the network no longer mints new lumens.
There is NO fee burning on Stellar — transaction fees go to the network fee pool and are not destroyed, unlike EIP-1559-style burn chains.
Because supply is fixed and fees aren't burned, circulating supply does not structurally shrink/deflate over time; it instead rises gradually as SDF-held lumens are released.
mustAvoid: Claiming XLM has an ongoing deflationary fee burn (EIP-1559-style) that shrinks supply over time.
Saying the ~1%/year protocol inflation is still active and still minting new lumens.
Asserting a hard Bitcoin-style minted max-supply cap rather than a fixed ~50B total after the 2019 burn.
passIf: The answer states total supply is effectively fixed near 50B after the 2019 SDF burn, that protocol inflation was disabled, that fees are NOT burned, and concludes supply is non-deflationary (doesn't structurally shrink).
failIf: It claims Stellar burns transaction fees / is deflationary.
It says XLM inflation is still active and the protocol still mints new lumens.
It badly misstates the supply numbers (e.g., max supply 105B today, or denies any burn happened).
inconclusiveIf: It mentions the ~50B figure or the burn but never addresses whether fees are burned / whether supply deflates.
It discusses inflation removal only and omits the no-fee-burn / non-deflationary pillar.
canonicalFacts: SDF burned ~55B XLM at Meridian on Nov 4–5, 2019, cutting total supply from ~105B to ~50B; total supply is now effectively fixed near 50 billion lumens (no Bitcoin-style minted cap, but no further burns pledged).
The original ~1%/year inflation mechanism was disabled in Stellar Core protocol v12 by validator vote (Oct 28, 2019); the protocol no longer mints new lumens.
Stellar does NOT burn transaction fees — fees are collected into the network fee pool and are not destroyed, so there is no ongoing deflationary burn.
Circulating supply does not structurally shrink; it rises gradually as SDF-held lumens are released from its mandate buckets (exact circulating figure varies over time).

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The fixed ~50B total supply, the 2019 burn, the disabling of inflation, and the absence of fee burning are stable historical/protocol facts; only the point-in-time circulating figure varies and isn't required.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable as a stable protocol/tokenomics fact (2019 burn, protocol v12 inflation disable, fee-pool behavior); no live data needed.
Any specific circulating-supply number should be framed as indicative and time-varying.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Noting fees are 'locked' in the fee pool rather than redistributed.
Mentioning the Oct 28, 2019 validator vote / Nov 2019 Meridian timing.
Clarifying the distinction between total supply and circulating supply.
niceToHave: Contrasts XLM's fixed-non-burning model with EIP-1559 burn chains and with Bitcoin's minted-cap model.
Notes the SDF's pledge not to burn additional lumens.

questions.question: What incentive do banks and institutions have to adopt Stellar and use XLM as a bridge asset, rather than just using a fiat stablecoin or their own internal ledger?

semantic.intent: Reframe the premise and explain the real institutional incentive: banks and institutions adopt Stellar as a network but settle in regulated stablecoins issued on it (not XLM), and Stellar wins over a generic chain or a closed internal ledger because of protocol-native institutional controls plus an open fiat anchor network. Correct the assumption that XLM is a mandatory bridge currency and explain XLM's actual role as the underlying network token.
mustInclude: The value-carrying instrument for institutions is regulated stablecoins issued on Stellar (e.g. USDC, PYUSD, EURC, or a bank's own coin), not XLM itself.

Stellar's edge over a generic chain or an internal ledger comes from protocol-native institutional controls (asset freeze/clawback, controlled/reversible transaction flows, compliance- and KYC-compatible operations) plus fast, low-cost, 24/7 public settlement.
An open, SWIFT-like anchor network provides fiat on/off-ramps that a closed internal ledger can't reach.
XLM is the network's native asset (paying tiny fees, anti-spam/minimum reserves, and optional path-payment/DEX bridge liquidity) and is not a required bridge currency between two fiats.
mustAvoid: Asserting that XLM must be used as the bridge/intermediary asset between two fiat currencies (the legacy 'XLM as the bridge' framing as the current institutional answer).
Claiming institutions hold or transact value in XLM rather than in stablecoins on Stellar.
Saying a closed internal ledger or a generic chain offers the same fiat-anchor reach and built-in compliance controls.
passIf: The answer settles institutional value in stablecoins on Stellar (not XLM), names at least the institutional-controls advantage (freeze/clawback/compliance) and the open anchor/fiat on-off-ramp reach, and correctly casts XLM as the native network token rather than a mandatory bridge currency.
failIf: It presents XLM as the required bridge asset between fiats as the real institutional incentive.
It says institutions adopt Stellar to transact in/hold XLM as the value-carrying asset.
It claims Stellar offers no institutional advantage over a stablecoin on a generic chain or an internal ledger.
inconclusiveIf: It correctly favors stablecoins on Stellar but never explains why Stellar beats a generic chain or an internal ledger (omits institutional controls and/or the open anchor network).
It discusses XLM's role vaguely without ruling out the 'XLM is the mandatory bridge currency' premise.
canonicalFacts: Institutions adopt Stellar (the network) and transact in regulated stablecoins issued on Stellar (USDC, PYUSD, EURC, or a bank's own coin) as the value-carrying asset, not in XLM.

Stellar's institutional draw over a generic chain or a closed internal ledger is protocol-native controls and economics: asset freeze/clawback, controlled/reversible transaction flows, enforceable compliance and KYC-compatible operations at the base layer (U.S. Bank cited these), plus settlement in seconds, ~$0.0007 per transaction (indicative, varies), and 24/7 public-ledger transparency.
An open, SWIFT-like anchor network provides fiat on/off-ramps that a closed internal ledger cannot reach.

XLM is not a mandatory bridge currency; its role is the native network asset — paying tiny fees, anti-spam/security/minimum reserves, and optional path-payment / DEX bridge liquidity between assets — so institutional stablecoin activity drives XLM demand only indirectly.

The live corpus shows stablecoins and tokenized RWAs as the actual settlement instruments (indicative figures: ~$246M stablecoins, ~$1.4B RWAs on Stellar; time-varying), with XLM as the underlying network token.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The institutional incentive structure — stablecoins as the settlement instrument, protocol-native controls, the anchor network, and XLM's role as native token — is stable; only point-in-time figures (per-tx cost, stablecoin/RWA totals) vary and are indicative.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the standards/assets skills and stable protocol facts (Stellar Asset Contract controls, anchors/SEPs, XLM's native-asset role); any cited dollar/throughput figures are indicative and time-varying.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Naming the U.S. Bank example as an institution that cited base-layer compliance/controls.
Mentioning tokenized RWAs on Stellar alongside stablecoins as settlement instruments.
Noting that the legacy 'XLM bridges two fiats' story still appears in older explainers.
niceToHave: Explains that rising institutional stablecoin activity drives XLM fee/liquidity demand only indirectly.
Distinguishes the network-vs-asset question clearly (adopt Stellar the network; settle in stablecoins on it).

questions.question: Why does my Stellar transfer or swap fail when my XLM balance is low, and is my money stuck in my wallet?

semantic.intent: Explain that the transfer/swap fails because every Stellar account must keep a minimum XLM balance reserve, so an operation that would drop the balance below it is rejected; and reassure the user that the reserved XLM is not stuck or lost and can be freed again.
mustInclude: Every account must hold a minimum balance equal to (2 + number of subentries) x the 0.5 XLM base reserve, so a fresh account locks at least 1 XLM and each subentry (trustline, offer, extra signer) reserves another 0.5 XLM.
The transfer/swap fails because the operation would take the balance below that minimum reserve, which is why you can't send 100% of your XLM and must leave the reserve plus the network fee.
The money is NOT stuck or lost: the reserved XLM stays in the account and becomes spendable again by removing the subentry holding it (close a trustline, cancel an offer) or by merging the account.
Reserves can also be covered by a sponsor so they don't come out of the account holder's own balance.
mustAvoid: Claiming the funds are permanently lost, locked by Stellar, or recoverable only by contacting support / customer service.
Saying you can spend 100% of your XLM, or omitting that a network fee must be left on top of the reserve.
Telling the user the fix is to send more XLM into the account with no mention that the reserve is recoverable by removing subentries or merging.
passIf: The answer attributes the failure to the minimum balance / base reserve, explains it scales with subentries above the ~1 XLM floor, and reassures that the reserved XLM is recoverable (close a trustline / cancel an offer / merge), not stuck.
failIf: It says the money is stuck, lost, or locked with no recovery path.
It does not connect the failure to the minimum balance / base reserve at all (e.g. blames it solely on a generic bug or fee).
It claims you can spend your entire XLM balance down to zero.
inconclusiveIf: It mentions a minimum balance or reserve but never reassures the user the XLM is recoverable / not stuck.
It reassures the money isn't lost but never explains the reserve mechanism that caused the failure.
canonicalFacts: A transfer/swap fails when it would drop the XLM balance below the account's minimum balance reserve; operations that would take you below it simply fail.

Minimum balance = (2 + number of subentries) x the base reserve of 0.5 XLM, so a brand-new account locks an absolute minimum of 1 XLM and each subentry (trustline, offer, additional signer) adds another 0.5 XLM that is reserved and not spendable.
The reserved XLM is not lost: it stays in the account and becomes spendable again once the subentry holding it is removed (close trustline / cancel offer) or the account is merged.
Reserves can be paid by a sponsor (CAP-33, since protocol 15) so they don't come from the account holder's own balance.
The fix for a failed transfer is to leave enough XLM above the minimum balance plus the small network fee (the 0.5 XLM base reserve is the current protocol value and could change via governance).

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The minimum-balance/base-reserve mechanism, subentry math, recoverability, and sponsorship are stable protocol facts; only the 0.5 XLM base-reserve value could change via future governance and is not a point-in-time market figure.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the assets/accounts skill as a stable protocol fact; no live chain query needed beyond the user's own account state.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Note that you must 'add' (establish a trustline for) an asset before you can receive it, which itself consumes a 0.5 XLM reserve.
Mention that account merge requires no subentries first and returns the full balance including reserves.
Practical guidance to keep a small XLM buffer above the reserve to cover fees.
niceToHave: Quantifies a concrete example (e.g. one trustline => 1.5 XLM minimum) to make the math tangible.
Distinguishes the recoverable reserve from the truly-spent network fee.

questions.question: Can I recover XLM or tokens that were stolen from my hacked or drained Stellar wallet, and how did the attacker get access?

semantic.intent: Tell a victim of a hacked/drained self-custody Stellar wallet, honestly, that their stolen funds are practically unrecoverable; explain why (transaction finality, and that the SDF cannot freeze or return funds in user accounts); identify how access was most likely gained (key/approval compromise, not a Stellar protocol bug); and give the realistic next steps.
mustInclude: Funds stolen from a self-custody wallet are practically unrecoverable because Stellar transactions are final and irreversible.
The Stellar Development Foundation has no control over user accounts and cannot freeze or return XLM held in a Stellar account.

Access is almost always gained by compromising the user's keys or approvals (phishing, SIM-swap of SMS 2FA, fake wallet apps harvesting the seed phrase, signature/approval phishing) rather than a Stellar protocol flaw.
The only realistic remedy is to report the theft to law enforcement (e.g. the FBI IC3) and move any remaining assets to a fresh wallet.
mustAvoid: Promising or implying the funds can be reversed, refunded, or recovered for an individual victim, or that paying a 'recovery service' will get them back.
Claiming the SDF (or any central party) can freeze the thief's account or return the XLM on request from the victim.
Blaming a Stellar protocol bug as the cause instead of key/approval/device compromise.
passIf: The answer states the theft is practically unrecoverable due to transaction finality, makes clear the SDF cannot freeze/return user funds, attributes the breach to key/approval compromise rather than a protocol flaw, and points to realistic next steps (report to law enforcement, move remaining assets to a fresh wallet).
failIf: It tells the victim the stolen funds can be reversed/recovered, or recommends a paid 'recovery' service.
It says the SDF or another authority can freeze the attacker and return the XLM at the victim's request.
It attributes the loss to a Stellar protocol/network bug rather than compromise of keys or approvals.
inconclusiveIf: It is sympathetic and on-topic but never states clearly that the funds are practically unrecoverable due to finality.
It gives generic 'be careful' advice without explaining either that the SDF cannot intervene or that the cause was key/approval compromise.
canonicalFacts: Stellar transactions are final and irreversible, so funds drained from a self-custody wallet are practically unrecoverable for an individual victim.
The SDF states it does not have access to or control over Stellar accounts and cannot freeze or return XLM held in a Stellar account.

Drains almost always result from compromised keys/approvals: phishing sites, SIM-swap of SMS 2FA, fake wallet apps harvesting seed phrases, signature/approval phishing, or wallet-generation/RNG flaws — not a Stellar protocol bug.
Realistic next steps are to report the theft to law enforcement (e.g. FBI IC3) and move any remaining assets to a fresh wallet.

A protocol-level exception exists but is not a user-invokable recovery path: a quorum of Stellar validators can freeze attacker-controlled accounts during a large exploit via CAP-77 'Quorum Freeze' (e.g. the Feb 2026 Blockaid/YieldBlox oracle attack where ~48M XLM was quarantined onchain), decided case-by-case as governance-coordinated incident response.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: Transaction finality, the SDF's stated lack of control over user accounts, and the typical compromise vectors are stable facts; no live lookup is needed to answer this honestly.

evidenceRequirements: Grounded in the SDF Security Team's published stance (no control over / cannot freeze user accounts) and durable protocol facts about transaction finality; the CAP-77 quorum-freeze exception is documented (and illustrated by the Feb 2026 YieldBlox incident) but is governance-decided, not a victim remedy.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Concrete prevention guidance (hardware wallet, never share the seed phrase, verify wallet app authenticity, prefer non-SMS 2FA).
Naming the CAP-77 quorum-freeze exception while making clear an individual victim cannot invoke it.
A caution about 'recovery service' scams that target prior victims.
niceToHave: An empathetic but honest tone that does not give false hope.
A short checklist of immediate steps (move remaining funds, revoke approvals, file an IC3 report).

questions.question: I got an email / DM saying I have a pending XLM claim, or that a bot flagged my account and I must pay to rectify it, or to re-sign at a site like xlm.services. Is this a Stellar phishing scam?

semantic.intent: Tell the user this is a textbook Stellar phishing scam, warn them to never reveal their secret/seed/recovery key, never pay or deposit anything, and never 're-sign' or sign a transaction on the linked site, and explain why the messages are fraudulent: SDF/Stellar cannot freeze, flag, or return XLM, never asks for keys or payments, never cold-DMs or emails security alerts, and no longer runs airdrops/giveaways/staking.
mustInclude: Identifies it as a phishing scam and tells the user not to act on the email/DM/link.
Never share or enter your secret key, seed, or recovery phrase, and never 're-sign' or sign a transaction on the linked site (e.g. xlm.services).
Never pay, deposit, or send funds to 'rectify' an account or 'claim' a pending balance.

SDF/Stellar cannot freeze, flag, or return XLM and never asks for keys, never asks for payments, never cold-contacts users, and no longer runs airdrops/giveaways/staking; verify only the real stellar.org domain and ignore unsolicited claimable balances.
mustAvoid: Suggesting the user comply in any way — entering/'re-signing' their key, paying a fee, or visiting/connecting their wallet to the linked site to 'check' or 'resolve' it.
Implying the message could be legitimate, that SDF can freeze/flag/return XLM, or that Stellar runs official staking/airdrops/giveaways.
passIf: The answer calls it a phishing scam and clearly tells the user not to share their secret/seed key, not to pay anything, and not to 're-sign'/sign on the linked site.
It conveys that SDF/Stellar never asks for keys or payments and cannot freeze/flag/return XLM, so the message is fraudulent.
failIf: It treats the message as possibly legitimate or advises following the link, paying the fee, or 're-signing'/connecting the wallet to resolve it.
It tells the user to enter or share their secret key/seed phrase, or claims SDF can flag/freeze/return XLM or runs official staking/airdrops.
inconclusiveIf: It vaguely warns 'be careful of scams' but does not clearly say not to share the secret key, not to pay, and not to sign on the linked site.
It is on-topic but omits the key reason (SDF never asks for keys/payments and cannot freeze/flag/return XLM).
canonicalFacts: These 'pending XLM claim' / 'bot flagged your account, pay to rectify' / 're-sign at xlm.services' messages are textbook Stellar phishing scams designed to capture your seed phrase or get you to sign a draining transaction.

Per SDF's official security guidance: the Stellar Development Foundation NEVER asks for your private/secret keys, NEVER asks you to deposit or pay funds, NEVER cold-DMs or emails support/security alerts, and no longer runs staking, airdrops, or giveaways.
SDF cannot freeze, 'flag', or return XLM in any account — so any message claiming it can is fraudulent.

Verify the real domain: only https://www.stellar.org is legitimate (anything other than a '/' right after stellar.org is a giveaway); ignore unsolicited claimable balances you don't understand; report scams to abuse@stellar.org and the FBI IC3 (ic3.gov).
This pattern is documented in Stellar's history, e.g. the 2020 'Stellar Staking Marathon' email-phishing campaign (illustrative example).

freshnessPolicy.rationale: This is stable security guidance — SDF never asks for keys or payments, cannot freeze/flag/return XLM, and runs no airdrops/staking; the scam pattern and safe response do not depend on live data.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from stable Stellar security facts and SDF's official 'avoiding scams' guidance; no live lookup needed.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Naming reporting channels (abuse@stellar.org, ic3.gov).
The specific domain-check heuristic ('/' must follow stellar.org).
A historical example such as the 2020 Stellar Staking Marathon phishing campaign.
niceToHave: Notes that unsolicited claimable balances can themselves be bait and should be ignored rather than interacted with.
Reassures that simply receiving the message does not put funds at risk unless the user shares keys, pays, or signs.

questions.question: Is it safe to store an encrypted Stellar secret key (or its hash) on-chain, in a custodial database, or in a password manager for recovery — and can you recover a key from its hash?

semantic.intent: Warn the user against treating on-chain, custodial-DB, or password-manager storage of a Stellar secret key as a safe recovery mechanism, explain why each is a trust/exposure tradeoff, clarify that a key cannot be recovered from its hash because hashing is one-way, and point to the recommended recovery alternatives (SEP-30 recoverysigner multisig, hardware wallets/passkeys) instead of storing a backup secret.
mustInclude: Storing a secret key on-chain is unsafe: anything written to the public ledger is permanently public, so the encrypted key is only as strong as its passphrase and is exposed to offline cracking forever; whoever recovers the master key controls the account and its funds.

Custodial DB or password-manager storage is a trust / single-point-of-failure tradeoff, not safe recovery; the common fix of encrypting the key under a password the server never sees breaks recovery, because if the server can't decrypt it, it can't help a user who forgets the password.
You cannot recover a secret key from its hash: a cryptographic hash is one-way / irreversible, so a hash can only verify a key you already supply and storing only a hash gives no recovery path.
The recommended approach is to not store a backup secret at all — use Stellar multisig with SEP-30 recoverysigner (recovery servers re-sign via identity verification) or hardware wallets / passkeys.
mustAvoid: Claiming it's fine to store an encrypted secret key on-chain because the encryption protects it.
Suggesting that a key can be recovered, reversed, or regenerated from its hash.
Recommending a single custodial database or password manager as a safe primary recovery mechanism without flagging the trust / single-point-of-failure tradeoff.
passIf: The answer says no, frames on-chain/custodial/password-manager storage as a trust-and-exposure tradeoff rather than safe recovery, states a key cannot be recovered from a one-way hash, and points to SEP-30 recoverysigner multisig or hardware wallets/passkeys as the recommended alternative.
failIf: It says storing an encrypted secret key on-chain is safe.
It claims a secret key can be recovered/reversed from its hash.
It endorses a single custodial DB or password manager as safe recovery without naming the single-point-of-failure / trust tradeoff.
inconclusiveIf: It only says 'don't store secret keys' generically without addressing why on-chain storage is permanently public and offline-crackable, or without addressing the hash-recovery question.
It mentions a recovery alternative but never names SEP-30 recoverysigner or hardware wallets/passkeys, leaving the recommended path vague.
evidenceRequirements: Stable protocol/cryptography fact grounded in Stellar 'Key Management 101' guidance and the standards skill (SEP-30 recoverysigner); the one-way-hash and on-chain-permanence properties are durable.
No live data needed; this is not a point-in-time question.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The properties at issue — public-ledger permanence, one-way hashing, the encrypt-under-user-password recovery break, and SEP-30/hardware-wallet recovery patterns — are stable cryptographic and protocol facts that do not change over time.

canonicalFacts: Anything written on-chain is permanently public, so an encrypted secret key stored there is only as safe as its passphrase and is exposed to offline (and future quantum) cracking indefinitely; the master key alone controls the account and its funds.

Storing a key on a server/custodial DB means fully trusting that server; the common workaround of encrypting the key under a user password the server never sees breaks recovery, because a server that cannot decrypt the key also cannot help a user who forgets the password.

A cryptographic hash is one-way and irreversible by design: it can verify a key you already supply but can never regenerate the secret key, so storing only a hash provides no recovery path and still leaks a brute-force/precompute target.

The recommended modern approach is to not store a backup secret at all — use Stellar multisig with SEP-30 recoverysigner (independent recovery servers re-sign via identity verification) or hardware wallets / passkeys.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Noting the 'losing keys and losing passwords go hand in hand' tradeoff from Stellar's Key Management guidance.
Mentioning offline/quantum precompute as a long-horizon risk for anything published on a public ledger.
Suggesting client-side encryption of a backup is acceptable only when the user fully controls the passphrase and accepts no-recovery-on-loss.
niceToHave: Distinguishes 'verify a supplied key' (a legitimate use of a hash) from 'recover a key' (impossible).
Frames SEP-30 as splitting recovery across independent servers so no single party is a single point of failure.

questions.question: If I provide liquidity, use AMMs, or earn yield/lending on Stellar, what are the risks? Can I lose my principal, and what is impermanent loss?

semantic.intent: Educate a retail user that DeFi yield on Stellar is compensation for real, layered risk rather than free money, and that principal can genuinely be lost. A correct answer explains impermanent loss for AMM liquidity providers, names the smart-contract / oracle / shared-pool risks of lending and yield protocols, and advises vetting before depositing.
mustInclude: Explains impermanent loss: the value gap between holding two tokens vs depositing them in a constant-product AMM pool, which materializes when their price ratio diverges (worst in volatile markets, partly offset by trading fees).
States that principal CAN be lost in lending/yield protocols (e.g. Blend, YieldBlox, Slender) via smart-contract, oracle-manipulation, or shared-pool/collateral risk — not merely forgone yield.
Conveys Stellar's own guidance that third-party DeFi is used at your own risk and that losses can exceed your initial allocation.
Advises vetting collateral quality / market depth, oracle design, audit scope, and pool isolation before depositing, noting an audit does not cover every risk.
mustAvoid: Framing Stellar DeFi yield as risk-free, guaranteed, or principal-protected.
Claiming a formal audit (e.g. Certora) eliminates oracle/market-quality risk or guarantees depositors are made whole.
Reducing the risk to impermanent loss alone while ignoring smart-contract/oracle/collateral risk in lending.
passIf: The answer defines impermanent loss correctly AND states principal can be lost in lending/yield protocols via smart-contract/oracle/collateral risk, while framing yield as compensation for risk.
failIf: It says yield is safe / principal-protected, or that you can only lose forgone interest not principal.
It mischaracterizes impermanent loss (e.g. calls it a fee, or says it only happens if you withdraw at a loss unrelated to price-ratio divergence).
It asserts that an audit or formal verification removes oracle/market-manipulation risk.
inconclusiveIf: It mentions 'risk' generically but neither defines impermanent loss nor explains how lending/yield principal can be lost.
It covers only AMM/impermanent loss or only lending risk, missing the other key pillar.
evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the defi skill plus stable protocol facts; impermanent loss and the smart-contract/oracle/collateral risk categories are durable.
Specific incident figures (e.g. the YieldBlox/Blend-V2 ~$10M drain, ~$158K borrowed against ~$16M nominal, $125K Certora audit) are illustrative, not point-in-time live data.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: Impermanent loss, the at-your-own-risk DeFi posture, and the categories of lending/oracle/collateral risk are stable; only current APYs and specific incident totals vary and are not required for a correct answer.

canonicalFacts: Impermanent loss is per Stellar's docs the most significant AMM risk: the value gap between tokens deposited in a constant-product pool vs holding them in a wallet, which materializes when the two tokens' price ratio diverges (worst in volatile markets, offset by trading fees).
Lending/yield protocols on Stellar (Blend, YieldBlox, Slender) carry smart-contract, oracle, and shared-pool/collateral risk where principal can be lost, not just yield.

A formal audit does not cover every risk: in the Feb 2026 YieldBlox/Blend-V2 incident (~$10M drained, indicative) an attacker pumped a thinly-traded collateral asset on an illiquid SDEX market to poison the Reflector VWAP oracle and over-borrow, despite a $125K Certora audit that did not cover oracle/market-quality risk; depositors were later made whole but that was discretionary, not guaranteed.
Stellar's official guidance is that third-party DeFi is used at your own risk and 'Losses can exceed your initial allocation.'
allowedExtraNotRequired: Mention of slippage and price-manipulation risk for AMM swaps.
Reference to the specific YieldBlox/Blend-V2 oracle-manipulation incident as a concrete example.
Note that depositors being made whole after an exploit is discretionary, not a guarantee.
niceToHave: Distinguishes 'lose value vs holding' (impermanent loss) from 'outright loss of principal' (exploit/oracle/collateral) as different failure modes.
Notes that trading fees can partly or fully offset impermanent loss in low-volatility pairs.

questions.question: How do I find or view a list of all the anchors and tradable trustline assets on the Stellar network?

semantic.intent: Help a user discover anchors and assets on Stellar by pointing them at the right catalogs: the curated SDF anchor directory for interoperable on/off-ramp anchors, and a block explorer's asset directory for the complete on-chain list of issuers/tradable assets. A correct answer should explain why one source is curated while the other is comprehensive.
mustInclude: Points to the SDF/official anchor directory at resources.stellar.org/anchors as a searchable, filterable catalog of anchors (e.g. filter by asset, country, anchor type, or SEP standard).
Notes that the anchor directory is NOT comprehensive because Stellar is permissionless and anyone can issue an asset.
For the complete on-chain list of all issuers and tradable trustline assets, recommends a block explorer's asset directory such as stellar.expert/directory.
Frames anchors via the relevant SEP deposit/withdrawal standards (SEP-6 / SEP-24 / SEP-31).
mustAvoid: Claiming the SDF anchor directory is a complete/exhaustive list of every asset or issuer on Stellar.
Implying assets must be approved or whitelisted to exist on-chain (Stellar issuance is permissionless).
Inventing a single 'official registry of all assets' that does not exist instead of pointing to a block explorer.
passIf: The answer names the SDF anchor directory (resources.stellar.org/anchors) for curated anchors AND a block explorer asset directory (e.g. stellar.expert/directory) for the comprehensive on-chain asset/issuer list, and conveys that the anchor directory is not comprehensive because issuance is permissionless.
failIf: It presents the anchor directory as the complete list of all assets/issuers on Stellar.
It only describes how to find anchors and never gives a way to view the full set of on-chain trustline assets/issuers (or vice versa).
It claims there is no way to discover anchors or assets, or fabricates a non-existent central asset registry.
inconclusiveIf: It mentions discovering anchors or assets only vaguely, without naming a concrete catalog (anchor directory or block explorer asset directory).
It covers one pillar correctly (curated anchors OR comprehensive on-chain assets) but omits the other and the curated-vs-comprehensive distinction.
evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the standards skill / SDF docs; the curated-anchor vs permissionless-asset distinction and the SEP-6/24/31 framing are stable facts.
Specific catalog URLs (resources.stellar.org/anchors, stellar.expert/directory) and per-anchor coverage are best confirmed against live sources since listings change.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The two-tool approach and the permissionless-issuance rationale are stable, but which anchors are listed, country/coverage counts, and the exact directory URLs/listings change over time, so a live source confirms the current catalogs.

canonicalFacts: The SDF Anchor Directory at resources.stellar.org/anchors is a curated, searchable catalog of interoperable anchors, filterable by name, country, supported asset, anchor type, and SEP standard (SEP-6 / SEP-24 / SEP-31).
SDF's companion interactive coverage map (anchors.stellar.org) shows anchor reach across roughly 180 countries (count is indicative and varies).
The anchor directory is explicitly NOT comprehensive: Stellar is permissionless, so anyone can issue an asset and not every issuer/asset is listed there.
For the complete, on-chain list of all issuers and tradable trustline assets, use a block explorer's asset directory; SDF itself recommends stellar.expert/directory.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Mention of the anchors.stellar.org interactive coverage map.
Note that anchors typically expose their endpoints via a SEP-1 stellar.toml.
Caution that listing in a directory is not an endorsement and users should verify regulation/custody themselves.
niceToHave: Explicitly contrasts 'curated/interoperable anchors' versus 'comprehensive on-chain assets' so the user picks the right tool for their goal.
Distinguishes anchors (on/off-ramp services) from assets (issued tokens/trustlines) rather than conflating them.

questions.question: How do two anchors in different countries settle a cross-border payment on Stellar (currency conversion, path payments, inter-anchor agreements)?

semantic.intent: Explain how a cross-border payment between two anchors in different countries settles on Stellar as a single on-chain route — local fiat in at the source, on-chain currency conversion via the Stellar DEX path payment, local fiat out at the destination, with no correspondent bank — and name the SEP that standardizes the anchor-to-anchor leg.
mustInclude: The sending anchor takes the payer's local fiat and issues/holds an on-chain asset, while the receiving anchor redeems its own asset and pays out local fiat off-chain at the destination.

Currency conversion happens on-chain via a path payment routed through the Stellar DEX (order books / liquidity), finding the best-priced path between the two anchors' assets (e.g. a EUR-pegged asset to an NGN-pegged asset).
The anchor-to-anchor (B2B) settlement is standardized by SEP-31, distinct from a single user's own on/off-ramp (SEP-24); quotes/exchange rates come via SEP-38 and anchors apply an FX spread.
This replaces correspondent banking / SWIFT intermediaries; the off-chain payout plus KYC/compliance is the final settlement step.
mustAvoid: Claiming settlement requires a correspondent/intermediary bank or SWIFT to bridge the two currencies.
Saying conversion happens off-chain or via a single centralized FX desk rather than on-chain path payment through the DEX.
Conflating the anchor-to-anchor leg (SEP-31) with a single user's self-service on/off-ramp (SEP-24).
passIf: The answer describes fiat-in -> on-chain path-payment conversion across the Stellar DEX between the two anchors' assets -> fiat-out, with no correspondent bank, and names SEP-31 as the anchor-to-anchor standard.
failIf: It claims the two anchors settle through a correspondent bank / SWIFT or an off-chain FX intermediary instead of an on-chain path payment.
It says one user moves their own funds (SEP-24) and never identifies the inter-anchor B2B settlement model.
It omits the on-chain currency conversion entirely (no path payment / DEX / order-book conversion between the assets).
inconclusiveIf: It mentions anchors and cross-border payments but never explains the on-chain conversion mechanism (path payment via the DEX).
It describes the flow correctly but never names the standard governing the anchor-to-anchor leg (SEP-31) when asked about inter-anchor agreements.
canonicalFacts: Two anchors in different countries settle as a single on-chain path through the Stellar DEX with no correspondent bank: the sending anchor accepts local fiat and issues its asset, a path payment converts it to the destination anchor's asset, and the receiving anchor redeems and pays out local fiat off-chain.
Currency conversion is on-chain: a path payment sources the best-priced route through DEX order books / liquidity, converting one anchor's pegged asset into the other's (e.g. EUROT -> NGNT).
SEP-31 standardizes the anchor-to-anchor (B2B, "wire-transfer-like") leg; SEP-24 is one user moving their own funds on/off ramp; SEP-38 provides exchange-rate quotes and anchors apply an FX spread.
The off-chain fiat payout plus KYC/compliance/regulatory checks is the final settlement step; the model replaces correspondent banking / SWIFT intermediaries.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The settlement architecture (path payments, anchors, and SEP-31/24/38 roles) is a stable protocol/standards fact; only specific live exchange rates and named anchors vary, and none are required for a correct answer.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the standards skill (SEP-31/24/38) and the cross-border-payments concept; this is a stable protocol fact, not a point-in-time figure.
Specific asset codes (EUROT, NGNT) and anchor names (Tempo, Kauri) are illustrative examples, not required exact values.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Naming concrete example anchors/assets to illustrate the EUR->NGN flow.
Noting trustlines / SEP-38 quote negotiation mechanics or the role of liquidity providers in the DEX path.
Mentioning settlement speed/cost advantages over correspondent banking.
niceToHave: Distinguishing SEP-31 (B2B inter-anchor) from SEP-24 (consumer on/off-ramp) and SEP-38 (quotes) by role.
Noting that path payments atomically guarantee the destination amount or the transaction fails.

questions.question: How do cross-chain assets like BTC and ETH get moved into and out of Stellar? Besides fiat anchors, what open-source or turnkey bridge solutions exist for non-stablecoin cross-chain transfers?

semantic.intent: Explain how value crosses between Stellar and other chains for non-stablecoin assets like BTC/ETH, distinguishing fiat-anchor on/off-ramps (which issue Stellar tokens against off-network value) from actual cross-chain bridges, and name concrete open-source or turnkey bridge options the user can use today.
mustInclude: Distinguishes anchors (SEP-compliant on/off-ramps that issue Stellar tokens against off-network value) from cross-chain bridges that move BTC/ETH-class assets between chains.

Names at least one concrete bridge route for BTC/ETH onto Stellar, e.g. Starbridge (SDF's trust-minimized, bidirectional Stellar<>Ethereum bridge, open-source on GitHub) and/or Allbridge as a live turnkey multi-chain bridge.
Notes that native USDC moves cross-chain via Circle CCTP using 1:1 burn-and-mint (no wrapped/custodial assets), as distinct from bridging non-stablecoin assets.
Mentions an intent-based / bridge-free swap path (e.g. NEAR Intents) or multi-route bridge aggregation as an alternative for getting non-stablecoin assets in without wrappers.
mustAvoid: Claiming Stellar has no way to move non-stablecoin assets cross-chain, or that only fiat anchors exist.
Conflating anchors with bridges (e.g. describing a SEP anchor as if it were a trust-minimized cross-chain bridge for BTC/ETH).
Asserting CCTP wraps assets or uses a custodial bridge rather than 1:1 burn-and-mint of native USDC.
passIf: The answer separates anchors from cross-chain bridges and names at least one concrete non-stablecoin bridge route (Starbridge and/or Allbridge), ideally also distinguishing native-USDC CCTP burn-and-mint.
It correctly frames at least one open-source or turnkey option for getting BTC/ETH-class assets onto Stellar without misrepresenting custody/wrapping.
failIf: It says fiat anchors are the only path and omits any actual cross-chain bridge for BTC/ETH.
It treats an anchor as a BTC/ETH bridge, or invents a bridge not grounded in the corpus.
It mischaracterizes CCTP as wrapped/custodial bridging rather than native-USDC burn-and-mint.
inconclusiveIf: It mentions bridges generically but names no concrete open-source/turnkey route (no Starbridge, Allbridge, CCTP, or intent-based path).
It distinguishes anchors from bridges conceptually but gives no usable option for moving BTC/ETH onto Stellar today.
evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the assets skill plus live corpus/directory (Starbridge GitHub repo, Allbridge project slug, CCTP article, NEAR Intents AV); specific live bridge availability and supported-asset lists are time-varying.
The anchor-vs-bridge distinction and CCTP burn-and-mint model are stable design facts; the inventory of live bridges should be checked against current ecosystem data.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: Which bridges are live, what assets they support, and recent additions (Allbridge Next non-stablecoin routes, CCTP on Stellar, NEAR Intents) change over time, so the concrete inventory should come from current corpus/directory data even though the anchor-vs-bridge concept is stable.

canonicalFacts: Anchors are Stellar's SEP-compliant on/off-ramps that issue Stellar tokens against off-network value; they are distinct from cross-chain bridges that move BTC/ETH-class assets between chains.
Starbridge is SDF's trust-minimized, bidirectional Stellar<>Ethereum bridge, open-source at github.com/stellar/starbridge, transferring Stellar assets and Ethereum ERC-20 tokens.

Allbridge is a live turnkey bridge connecting Stellar to chains like Ethereum/Solana/Polygon; Allbridge Next bridges non-stablecoin assets (ETH, BNB, POL, SOL, TRX, XLM) and routes via Circle CCTP and LayerZero OFT.
Circle CCTP went live on Stellar in 2026 doing 1:1 burn-and-mint of native USDC across many chains with no wrapped assets or custodial bridge; supported-chain count is indicative and growing.

Intent-based bridge-free swaps via NEAR Intents can bring BTC/ETH/SOL/XRP into Stellar without wrappers; other ecosystem bridges include Spacewalk/Pendulum, Axelar, Tricorn, Styx, plus Rango/WOWMAX aggregators.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Mention of SDF's 2022 Stellar Bridge Bounty Program (up to $5M XLM, Allbridge among finalists).
Listing additional ecosystem bridges/aggregators (Spacewalk/Pendulum, Axelar, Tricorn, Styx, zkCross, Rango, WOWMAX).
A note that Stellar/SDF frames Stellar itself as an interoperability gateway.
niceToHave: Distinguishes trust-minimized bridges from turnkey/custodial-style routes by trust model.
Points the user to the open-source repo or directory slug so they can verify current support.

questions.question: How do I tell a fake/scam Stellar token from the real one, and how do I remove or close the trustline for it?

semantic.intent: Help a retail user distinguish a legitimate Stellar asset from a scam look-alike, and walk them through safely removing the scam asset's trustline. A correct answer must teach issuer-based identity (not code alone), concrete verification steps, and the prerequisite of a zero balance before closing the trustline via change_trust limit 0.
mustInclude: A Stellar asset is identified by its CODE plus the ISSUER account public key (and on Soroban its contract ID); the code alone is not unique, so scammers mint look-alike assets (e.g. a fake USDC) under a different issuer.

Verify legitimacy by confirming the issuer matches the official account and is published/verified via a stellar.toml at the issuer's home_domain (SEP-1) and/or listed in a block explorer like StellarExpert or the wallet's verified-asset list; be wary of unsolicited airdropped tokens.
To remove a scam trustline the asset balance must first be zero (sell or send it away); a trustline holding a nonzero balance cannot be removed.
Removing/deleting the asset in the wallet submits a change_trust operation setting the trustline limit to 0, which closes the trustline and frees the 0.5 XLM base reserve.
mustAvoid: Telling the user the token is legit (or safe to remove) based on the asset code alone, ignoring the issuer.
Claiming a trustline can be removed while it still holds a nonzero balance.
Advising the user to share or enter their secret key / seed phrase anywhere to verify or remove the asset.
passIf: The answer keys asset identity on code+issuer (scammers reuse codes under different issuers), gives at least one concrete verification path (issuer matches official + SEP-1 stellar.toml / explorer / wallet verified list), and explains that the balance must be zeroed before a change_trust limit-0 removes the trustline.
failIf: It says you can tell real from fake by the asset code/name alone, omitting the issuer entirely.
It says you can remove the trustline without first emptying a nonzero balance.
It instructs the user to reveal their secret key or follows the airdropped token's prompts to 'claim/unlock' it.
inconclusiveIf: It correctly explains scam detection (code+issuer) but never covers how to actually remove/close the trustline, or vice versa.
It mentions verifying the issuer and removing the asset only vaguely, without the zero-balance prerequisite or the change_trust limit-0 mechanism.
canonicalFacts: A Stellar asset's identity is its CODE + ISSUER account public key (and on Soroban its contract ID); the code is not unique, so a fake 'USDC' can exist under a different issuer than Circle's official one.

Legitimacy checks: confirm the issuer equals the official account, that the issuer publishes a stellar.toml at a verified home_domain (SEP-1), and that it is listed/verified in your wallet or a block explorer like StellarExpert; treat unsolicited airdropped tokens as suspicious.
SDF's security guidance warns about look-alike assets and notes SDF never DMs users, asks for secret keys, or controls/freezes funds.
A trustline with a nonzero balance cannot be removed; the asset must be sold or sent away to reach zero first.

Deleting/removing the asset in a wallet (LOBSTR, Freighter, etc.) submits a change_trust operation with limit 0, closing the trustline and refunding the 0.5 XLM base reserve (indicative; reserve is a network parameter).

freshnessPolicy.rationale: Asset identity (code+issuer), SEP-1 verification, the zero-balance prerequisite, and change_trust limit-0 closing a trustline are stable protocol facts; only the specific official issuer key for a given asset and the base-reserve value can change, and the latter is a slow-moving network parameter.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the assets skill and stable Stellar protocol facts (asset model, trustlines, change_trust, SEP-1); the official issuer for a named asset should be confirmed against the explorer/wallet verified list rather than asserted from memory.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Naming specific wallets (LOBSTR, Freighter) and where their remove/delete-asset action lives.
Noting that the 0.5 XLM base reserve is a network parameter that can change.
Cautioning against interacting with or trying to 'claim' an airdropped scam token at all.
niceToHave: Mentions on-chain DEX selling or sending the dust away as the practical way to zero a nonzero balance before removal.
References SDF's security guide / 'look-alike assets' warning and the never-share-secret-key rule.

questions.question: My wallet's send or swap button is disabled, or my XLM transfer fails on-chain with 'recipient does not accept asset'. Why, and how do I fix it?

semantic.intent: Explain that a disabled send/swap button or an on-chain 'recipient does not accept asset' / op_no_trust failure is almost always a missing trustline: a Stellar account cannot receive or hold a non-native asset until it opts in via a ChangeTrust operation to that asset's code + issuer, and that the 0.5 XLM-per-trustline base reserve can independently block the action. Tell the user how to fix it.
mustInclude: A trustline (ChangeTrust to the asset code + the issuer's G-address) must be established before an account can hold or receive a non-native asset.
Without the trustline the send/swap fails on-chain (op_no_trust / 'recipient does not accept asset') and wallets gray out or disable the send/swap button.
Each trustline raises the account's minimum XLM balance by a 0.5 XLM base reserve, so insufficient reserve/spendable balance can also block adding the trustline or completing the transfer.
Fix: add/establish the trustline for that asset (and fund the small XLM reserve) before sending, swapping, or receiving it.
mustAvoid: Claiming a trustline is needed for native XLM transfers, or that XLM itself requires opting in.
Telling the user to add a trustline to an arbitrary/unverified issuer without noting that the issuer's G-address must match the intended asset.
Attributing the failure to network congestion, a buggy wallet, or insufficient general 'gas' rather than the missing trustline / reserve.
passIf: The answer identifies the missing trustline as the cause, names ChangeTrust (asset code + issuer), and tells the user to add the trustline before sending/receiving.
It also notes the 0.5 XLM base reserve per trustline as a possible secondary blocker.
failIf: It says native XLM needs a trustline, or that no opt-in is ever required for non-native assets.
It misidentifies the cause as something other than the trustline/reserve (e.g., congestion, gas, generic wallet bug).
It gives no actionable fix (never tells the user to establish the trustline).
inconclusiveIf: It mentions trustlines in passing but neither explains the ChangeTrust opt-in mechanic nor gives a concrete fix.
It correctly says 'add a trustline' but omits both the issuer/asset-code detail and the reserve so the user can't actually act.
canonicalFacts: A Stellar account must establish a trustline (ChangeTrust operation specifying the asset code and the issuer's G-address) before it can hold or receive that non-native asset; native XLM needs no trustline.
Sending or swapping into an asset the recipient (or the user's own account) has no trustline for fails on-chain — the op_no_trust / 'recipient does not accept asset' error class.
Wallets typically gray out or disable the send/swap button when the required trustline, or the XLM reserve to create it, is missing.

Each trustline raises the account's minimum XLM balance by a 0.5 XLM base reserve (indicative protocol value), so a too-low reserve or spendable balance can itself block adding the trustline or completing the send.
The fix is to add the trustline for that asset and fund the small XLM reserve before sending, swapping, or receiving it.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: Trustlines, the ChangeTrust opt-in requirement, the op_no_trust failure mode, and the per-trustline base reserve are stable protocol-level facts; the 0.5 XLM reserve is a long-standing network parameter and no live lookup is needed to answer.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable as a stable Stellar protocol fact from the assets skill (trustlines, ChangeTrust, base reserves); no live chain data required.
The 0.5 XLM base reserve is a network parameter — cite as indicative; the requirement to hold a trustline is invariant.
allowedExtraNotRequired: How to add a trustline in a specific wallet (e.g., Freighter 'Manage Assets' / add asset flow).
Note that the sender can also use a path payment that creates/uses an existing trustline, or that the recipient must add the trustline first.
Mention that removing an unused trustline frees the 0.5 XLM reserve back to spendable.
niceToHave: Distinguishes the 'recipient has no trustline' case from the 'my own account lacks the reserve' case as two distinct triggers for a disabled button.
Notes that the issuer G-address must be the correct/intended one to avoid trusting a look-alike asset.

questions.question: What are the tax implications of spending or cashing out XLM (capital gains, cost basis, converting to another crypto first)?

semantic.intent: Explain that disposing of XLM has tax consequences and frame it as general, non-advisory information. A correct answer must convey that both spending and cashing out XLM are taxable disposals computed as proceeds minus cost basis, that converting XLM into another crypto first does not defer or avoid the tax (the conversion is itself a taxable disposal — no like-kind exemption for crypto), and it should flag that this is general information, not tax advice, and that rules are jurisdiction-specific. Bonus: point to ecosystem tax tooling rather than authoring tax advice.
mustInclude: Spending or cashing out XLM is a taxable disposal: capital gain/loss = proceeds minus cost basis.
Converting XLM to another crypto is itself a taxable disposal — it does NOT defer or avoid tax (no like-kind exemption for crypto in the US).
Holding period determines short- vs long-term treatment; US custodial exchanges now report disposals (gross proceeds + cost basis) to the IRS (Form 1099-DA).
This is general info, not tax advice — outcomes are jurisdiction-specific; consult a qualified professional. Ecosystem tax tools like LedgersTax and Kryptos can compute Stellar taxable events.
mustAvoid: Claiming that converting XLM to another crypto first defers or avoids tax, or that a like-kind/1031 exemption applies to crypto.
Asserting a definitive jurisdiction-specific tax outcome as binding advice rather than general information.
Saying spending XLM (vs cashing out to fiat) is not a taxable event.
passIf: The answer frames both spending and cashing out as taxable disposals (proceeds minus cost basis), states that converting to another crypto is itself a taxable disposal and does not defer tax, and adds a not-tax-advice / consult-a-professional disclaimer.
failIf: It claims converting XLM to another crypto avoids or defers the tax, or invokes a like-kind exemption for crypto.
It treats spending XLM on goods/services as non-taxable while cashing out is taxable.
It presents a jurisdiction-specific result as definitive tax advice with no disclaimer.
inconclusiveIf: It says disposing of XLM is taxable but never addresses the crypto-to-crypto conversion (the core of the question) or omits cost basis.
It gives the disclaimer and tooling pointers but never states the actual disposal/cost-basis mechanics.
canonicalFacts: Spending or cashing out XLM is a disposal that triggers a capital gain or loss equal to proceeds minus cost basis.
Converting XLM into another crypto is itself a taxable disposal of the XLM; there is no like-kind exemption for crypto in the US, so it does not defer or avoid the tax.
Holding period determines short- vs long-term capital-gains treatment; US custodial exchanges now report disposals (gross proceeds and cost basis) to the IRS on Form 1099-DA.

Ecosystem tooling can compute these events: LedgersTax (ledgers.tax, SCF #35, slug ledgerstax) exports full Stellar transaction history as taxable-events reports (Koinly/TurboTax/CoinLedger-compatible), and Kryptos (kryptos.io, slug kryptos) does crypto tax reporting and cost-basis tracking with Stellar support.
This is general (US-centric) crypto-tax knowledge, not LumenLoop-authored tax advice; specifics vary by jurisdiction.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The disposal/cost-basis mechanics and the no-like-kind-for-crypto rule are stable; 1099-DA reporting is established. Exact rates, thresholds, and jurisdiction rules vary but the answer is general, not a live figure.

evidenceRequirements: Disposal and cost-basis mechanics and the no-like-kind-for-crypto point are stable, general US crypto-tax facts, not LumenLoop-authored guidance.
The ecosystem tooling pointers (LedgersTax, Kryptos) are grounded in the LumenLoop directory.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Mentioning loss harvesting or that capital losses can offset gains.
Noting that cost basis can use methods like FIFO/specific identification.
Naming non-US treatment differences as an example of jurisdiction dependence.
niceToHave: Pointing to LedgersTax/Kryptos rather than authoring tax advice.
Distinguishing the two disposals in a crypto-to-crypto swap (dispose XLM, acquire the new asset at its fair value as fresh basis).

questions.question: How does asset clawback work on Stellar, and how is it different from the authorization-revocable freeze flag or a credit-card chargeback?

semantic.intent: Explain Stellar's asset clawback as a first-class, issuer-only primitive that lets an asset issuer recover/burn tokens from a holder's balance, that it is opt-in and transparent (a clawback-enabled flag the issuer sets via Set Options BEFORE issuing, visible on the public ledger and on each trustline so holders accept it knowingly), and clearly distinguish it both from the authorization-revocable freeze (which only de-authorizes/freezes a trustline so tokens can't move, rather than removing them) and from a credit-card chargeback (a third-party/bank reversal after the fact), framing clawback as purpose-built for issuers of regulated/compliance assets.
mustInclude: Clawback lets an asset issuer recover (burn/remove) tokens from a holder's balance; it is an issuer-only operation, not something any account can do.

It is opt-in and transparent: the issuer enables a clawback-enabled flag (via Set Options, before issuing) and it is visible on the public ledger / on each trustline, so holders accept clawback-enabled assets knowingly.

It is distinct from the authorization-revocable flag, which only freezes/de-authorizes a trustline so tokens cannot move (revoke vs remove); and distinct from a chargeback, which is a third-party/bank reversal of a transaction after the fact.
It is designed for issuers of regulated assets (e.g. stablecoins, bonds, equities, money-market funds / RWA) to meet compliance requirements.
mustAvoid: Claiming any account (not just the issuer) can claw back tokens, or that clawback can be applied retroactively to assets issued without the clawback flag set first.
Conflating clawback with the freeze/revocation flag — i.e. saying clawback merely freezes a trustline rather than actually removing/burning the tokens.
Describing clawback as a chargeback-style or bank/third-party reversal, or as an EVM-style protocol-level seizure rather than an issuer-consented, flag-gated primitive.
passIf: The answer states clawback is an issuer-only recover/burn operation, notes it is opt-in/transparent via a pre-issuance clawback flag visible on-chain, and correctly contrasts it with the revocation/freeze flag (revoke vs remove) and with a chargeback (third-party reversal).
failIf: It says clawback freezes a trustline rather than removing tokens, or otherwise equates clawback with the authorization-revocable flag.
It claims any account can claw back, that no opt-in flag is needed, or that it can be applied to already-issued non-clawback assets.
It characterizes clawback as a chargeback / bank reversal or a generic protocol seizure rather than an issuer-consented Stellar primitive.
inconclusiveIf: It describes clawback as issuer token recovery but never contrasts it against the freeze/revocation flag or a chargeback (the comparison the question asks for).
It mentions clawback and freeze vaguely without making clear the revoke-vs-remove distinction or the opt-in flag.
canonicalFacts: Asset Clawback (Stellar Protocol 17, CAP-0035; indicative go-live June 1, 2021) lets an asset issuer recover/burn tokens from any holder of that asset; it adds the Clawback and Clawback Claimable Balance operations.

Clawback is gated by an account-level 'Authorization clawback-enabled' flag the issuer sets via Set Options BEFORE issuing; it is opt-in and transparent, visible on the public ledger and on each trustline (is_clawback_enabled: true), and existing balances of already-issued assets are unaffected.

The older revocation mechanism — the 'Authorization revocable' flag plus Set Trust Line Flags (which replaced the legacy Allow Trust op) — lets an issuer freeze/de-authorize a trustline so tokens cannot move, whereas clawback actually removes the tokens.

Unlike a credit-card chargeback (a bank reverses a third-party transaction after the fact) or an EVM-style protocol seizure, Stellar clawback is a first-class, issuer-only, consent-flagged primitive built so issuers of regulated assets (money-market funds, bonds, equities, stablecoins) can meet compliance requirements.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: Clawback is a stable protocol feature (Protocol 17 / CAP-0035) whose mechanics, flags, and distinction from revocation and chargebacks do not change; no live data is needed.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the assets skill / stable Stellar protocol facts (CAP-0035, Set Options clawback flag, Set Trust Line Flags); no live source required.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Naming the two operations (Clawback and Clawback Claimable Balance) and the is_clawback_enabled trustline field.
Noting the historical context that Set Trust Line Flags replaced the legacy Allow Trust operation.
Mentioning that already-issued (pre-flag) balances are not retroactively clawback-enabled.
niceToHave: Frames the 'eyes wide open' consent angle — holders opt into clawback-enabled assets knowingly because the flag is on-chain.
Contrasts revoke-vs-remove crisply in one line.

questions.question: How does the Stellar transaction fee work — is it fixed, does it scale with usage, and how does surge pricing affect it?

semantic.intent: Explain that Stellar's transaction fee is dynamic and market-based rather than a fixed protocol constant: it is charged per operation against a low network minimum/base fee, and rises via surge pricing when submitted operations exceed per-ledger capacity, where higher fee-per-operation bids win inclusion.
mustInclude: The fee is dynamic/market-based, NOT a fixed constant; you specify a maximum fee and are charged only the minimum needed for inclusion.

It is charged per operation (a transaction bundles multiple operations), against a low network minimum/base fee that is a fraction of a US cent (historically ~100 stroops = 0.00001 XLM per operation).
Surge pricing kicks in when the volume of submitted operations exceeds the per-ledger capacity limit.
Under surge pricing transactions act as bids: the highest fee-per-operation are prioritized for inclusion while the rest are deferred to a later ledger or dropped.
mustAvoid: Claiming Stellar's fee is a fixed flat amount per transaction set by the protocol.
Saying surge pricing makes fees arbitrarily expensive or implying you always pay your stated maximum rather than the minimum needed.
Confusing per-operation pricing with per-transaction pricing (ignoring that one transaction bundles multiple operations).
passIf: The answer states fees are dynamic/market-based and per-operation, names the low base/minimum fee, and explains surge pricing as a capacity-triggered bid where the highest fee-per-operation wins inclusion.
failIf: It says the Stellar fee is a fixed protocol constant with no dynamic/surge component.
It describes surge pricing wrong (e.g. a fixed multiplier, or claims all over-capacity transactions are permanently rejected) or claims you always pay the maximum you set.
inconclusiveIf: It mentions a low/cheap fee but never distinguishes per-operation pricing or explains the surge-pricing bid mechanism.
It says fees can rise under load but omits that this is capacity-triggered and resolved by highest fee-per-operation bids winning inclusion.
canonicalFacts: Stellar's transaction fee is dynamic and market-based, not a fixed protocol constant.

Fees are charged per operation (a transaction bundles 1-100 operations), against a network minimum/base fee set by validator consensus (historically 100 stroops = 0.00001 XLM per operation, a fraction of a US cent; the exact value can change).
Surge pricing activates when submitted operations exceed the per-ledger capacity limit (indicatively ~1,000 operations per ledger, ~5s close time — values are network parameters that can change).
Under surge pricing transactions act as bids: highest fee-per-operation are prioritized for inclusion and the rest are pushed to a later ledger or discarded.
You specify a maximum fee and are charged only the minimum needed to make the ledger; since Protocol 13 fee-bump transactions let a payer raise the fee on an already-signed transaction.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The mechanism (dynamic per-operation fees, a low base fee, and bid-based surge pricing on capacity overflow) is a stable protocol fact; only specific parameter values like the base fee in stroops or the per-ledger operation limit vary over time and should be framed as indicative.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from the standards skill / SDF developer docs on dynamic fees and surge pricing; the base-fee value and per-ledger operation limit are network parameters that should be cited as indicative/time-varying.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Mention of fee-bump transactions (Protocol 13) for raising the fee on an already-signed transaction.
Noting validators set the network minimum/base fee by consensus.
The stroop unit definition (1 XLM = 10,000,000 stroops).
niceToHave: Frames typical fees as trivially small (fractions of a US cent) to convey real-world cost.
Notes that deferred transactions can be retried in a later ledger once congestion clears.

questions.question: Does XLM need to have a high market value for the Stellar network to keep working? And how does XLM actually accrue value as a bridge/utility token?

semantic.intent: Reassure a retail user that Stellar does NOT depend on XLM having a high or significant market price to function, explain the protocol mechanics that only require tiny per-account/per-transaction amounts of XLM, and explain that XLM accrues utility (as a bridge/medium-of-exchange asset providing liquidity) rather than protocol/staking yield.
mustInclude: Stellar does NOT need XLM to have a high or significant market value to function; XLM is only required in tiny amounts per account/transaction.

Each account must hold a small minimum (base) reserve and every transaction pays a tiny fee (e.g. 0.00001 XLM / 100 stroops), designed as anti-spam friction rather than a price-dependent security budget.

XLM accrues UTILITY rather than staking/protocol yield: it acts as a universal medium of exchange / bridge asset that provides liquidity between otherwise-illiquid pairs via path payments on Stellar's built-in DEX.
Supply is fixed (~50B lumens; inflation was ended by community vote in 2019, no mining), and price/demand is driven by real usage (payments, stablecoin settlement, RWA) and can be volatile.
mustAvoid: Claiming the network's security or operation requires XLM to reach or hold a high price (a price-dependent security budget).
Saying XLM earns native staking / proof-of-stake / inflation rewards as a way to accrue value.
Asserting the lumen supply still inflates or is mined.
passIf: The answer says the network does not depend on a high XLM price, explains the tiny base-reserve + per-tx fee as anti-spam friction, and frames XLM's value as utility/bridge-and-liquidity (not protocol yield).
failIf: It states Stellar needs a high/significant XLM market value to keep working or to stay secure.
It claims XLM accrues value through native staking/PoS or ongoing inflation rewards.
It says XLM is mined or that the supply still inflates.
inconclusiveIf: It vaguely calls XLM a 'utility token' without either stating the network is price-independent or naming the bridge/path-payment liquidity mechanism.
It mentions the small fees/reserves but never addresses how XLM actually accrues value (bridge/utility vs yield).
canonicalFacts: Stellar does not require XLM to have a high or significant market value to function; the protocol only uses XLM in tiny per-account/per-transaction amounts.

Every account holds a small base reserve (historically 1 XLM) and pays a very small per-transaction fee (~0.00001 XLM / 100 stroops) as deliberate anti-spam friction, not a price-dependent security budget.
XLM serves as a universal medium of exchange / bridge asset, supplying liquidity between otherwise-illiquid pairs through path payments on Stellar's built-in DEX (XLM/USDC is a dominant pair).
Supply is fixed at ~50B lumens; inflation was ended by community vote in Oct 2019 and there is no mining.
Demand/value is driven by usage (payments, stablecoin settlement, RWA growth) and the price is acknowledged as volatile; figures are indicative and vary.

freshnessPolicy.rationale: The protocol mechanics (base reserve, per-tx fee as anti-spam friction, fixed supply, utility-not-yield accrual) are stable facts; only the live XLM price and usage figures vary and are not needed to answer correctly.

evidenceRequirements: Answerable from stable protocol facts (base reserve, fee schedule, supply, path payments) and the LumenLoop corpus (stellar.org/learn/lumens, article id 49); specific prices/usage figures are indicative and time-varying.
allowedExtraNotRequired: Note that a native (non-fiat) asset was chosen so the network favors no national currency and stays independent of economic/political factors.
Mention that Stellar ranks highly by RWA market cap as evidence of usage-driven demand.
Quote stellar.org that lumens provide utility but the price can be volatile.
niceToHave: Explicitly contrasts utility-driven value with proof-of-stake/mining-driven value to pre-empt the misconception.
Gives concrete fee numbers in both XLM and stroops to convey just how small the per-account/per-tx cost is.

questions.question: How does a Stellar path payment settle a cross-currency transfer when there's no direct fiat pair, and is XLM's conversion rate market-driven?

semantic.intent: Explain that a Stellar path payment settles a cross-currency transfer with no direct pair by routing the value through one or more intermediary 'bridge' assets (e.g. XLM or USDC), filling each hop against DEX order-book offers and AMM liquidity pools, executing atomically, with a market-determined conversion rate rather than a fixed or oracle rate.
mustInclude: The transfer routes through a chain of intermediary/bridge assets (XLM or another token) when no direct pair exists, consuming offers across hops.
Each hop is filled against the Stellar DEX order books and/or AMM liquidity pools, not a fixed or oracle-quoted rate.

The whole multi-hop conversion is atomic: sender debits one asset and receiver is credited another in a single operation/ledger, or the entire transaction fails (protected by strict-send/strict-receive minimums).
XLM's (and every leg's) conversion rate is market-driven, set by traders and market makers via supply and demand.
mustAvoid: Claiming XLM's conversion rate is fixed, pegged, or set by an oracle/protocol rather than the market.
